# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Sensitivity Analysis. What are its two principal applications in Operations Research?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensitivity Analysis** (also called **Post-Optimality Analysis**) is a structured mathematical procedure that studies how the optimal solution of a Linear Programming problem responds to changes in the model parameters (objective coefficients, RHS constants, or constraint coefficients) **without re-solving the entire problem**.

**Two Principal Applications:**

1. **Range of Optimality:** Determines the interval over which an objective function coefficient $c_j$ can vary while the **current optimal basis remains unchanged**. This guides a manager in evaluating how robust the optimal product mix is to profit/cost fluctuations.

2. **Range of Feasibility & Shadow Prices:** Determines the interval over which an RHS constant $b_i$ can vary while the **current basis remains feasible**, and computes the **Shadow Price** — the marginal increase in the optimal objective per unit increase in the constrained resource. This supports decisions on whether to procure additional resources.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two applications — total 3 marks.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by the "Shadow Price" of a resource constraint? Mention its physical interpretation in a civil engineering project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Shadow Price** ($y_i$) of the $i$-th resource constraint is defined as the **rate of change of the optimal objective function value $Z^*$ per unit change in the RHS constant $b_i$**, with all other parameters held constant. Mathematically:

$$
y_i = \frac{\partial Z^*}{\partial b_i} = \mathbf{c}_B^{\top} B^{-1} \mathbf{e}_i
$$

**Physical Interpretation in Civil Engineering:**

In a construction project, if the shadow price of "crane-hours per day" is ₹8,000 per hour, it implies that the project profit will **increase by ₹8,000 for every additional hour** of crane time made available, **as long as** the additional hours fall within the **range of feasibility**. The contractor should therefore be willing to pay **up to ₹8,000/hour** to rent an extra hour of crane time.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for mathematical definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

A construction firm produces two types of pre-cast panels. The LP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } & Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10 \\
& x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 12 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The **final optimal simplex tableau** (after introducing slacks $s_1, s_2$) is:

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 4 | 1 | 0 | 3/5 | $-1/5$ | 18/5 |
| $x_2$ | 3 | 0 | 1 | $-1/5$ | 2/5 | 14/5 |
| $Z_j$ | — | 4 | 3 | 9/5 | 2/5 | $Z^* = 114/5$ |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | $-9/5$ | $-2/5$ | — |

**Answer the following:**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**(i)** Extract the inverse basis matrix $B^{-1}$. **(ii)** Compute the **Shadow Prices** of both constraints. **(iii)** Verify that $\mathbf{y}^{\top} \mathbf{b} = Z^*$.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**(i)** Find the **allowable increase and decrease** in $b_1$ (currently $b_1 = 10$). **(ii)** If $b_1$ increases to $b_1 = 12$, compute the new optimal value of $Z$ using the shadow price approximation. Is the solution still feasible? Justify.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**(i) Extracting $B^{-1}$:** The columns under $s_1$ and $s_2$ in the final tableau:

$$
B^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 3/5 & -1/5 \\ -1/5 & 2/5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(ii) Computing Shadow Prices:**

$$
\mathbf{y}^{\top} = \mathbf{c}_B^{\top} B^{-1} = (4, 3) \begin{pmatrix} 3/5 & -1/5 \\ -1/5 & 2/5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Computing each element:

$$
y_1 = (4)(3/5) + (3)(-1/5) = 12/5 - 3/5 = 9/5 = 1.8
$$

$$
y_2 = (4)(-1/5) + (3)(2/5) = -4/5 + 6/5 = 2/5 = 0.4
$$

**Shadow Prices:** $y_1 = 1.8$, $y_2 = 0.4$.

**(iii) Verification:**

$$
\mathbf{y}^{\top} \mathbf{b} = (1.8)(10) + (0.4)(12) = 18 + 4.8 = 22.8
$$

Optimal $Z^* = 114/5 = 22.8$. ✓ **Verified.**

#### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Allowable Range for $b_1$:**

New basic solution with $\Delta b_1$:

$$
\mathbf{x}_B^{\text{new}} = B^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} 10 + \Delta b_1 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (3/5)(10 + \Delta b_1) + (-1/5)(12) \\ (-1/5)(10 + \Delta b_1) + (2/5)(12) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 18/5 + (3/5)\Delta b_1 \\ 14/5 - (1/5)\Delta b_1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Feasibility constraints** (each component $\geq 0$):

- $18/5 + (3/5)\Delta b_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta b_1 \geq -6$
- $14/5 - (1/5)\Delta b_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta b_1 \leq 14$

**Allowable increase:** $\Delta b_1^+ = 14$ → $b_1^{\max} = 10 + 14 = 24$.
**Allowable decrease:** $\Delta b_1^- = 6$ → $b_1^{\min} = 10 - 6 = 4$.

So $b_1 \in [4, 24]$ for feasibility.

**(ii) New $Z$ for $b_1 = 12$:**

$\Delta b_1 = 12 - 10 = 2$. Since $2 \in [-6, 14]$, the basis is **still feasible**.

$$
Z^{\text{new}} = Z^* + y_1 \Delta b_1 = 22.8 + (1.8)(2) = 22.8 + 3.6 = 26.4
$$

**Verification via direct computation:**

$$
\mathbf{x}_B^{\text{new}} = \begin{pmatrix} 18/5 + (3/5)(2) \\ 14/5 - (1/5)(2) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 24/5 \\ 12/5 \end{pmatrix} = (4.8, 2.4)
$$

$$
Z^{\text{new}} = 4(4.8) + 3(2.4) = 19.2 + 7.2 = 26.4 \quad ✓
$$

The solution is feasible (both $x_1 = 4.8 > 0$ and $x_2 = 2.4 > 0$).

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

A contractor manufactures concrete blocks ($x_1$) and bricks ($x_2$). The LP is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } & Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & 6x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 24 \\
& x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer the following:**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
Solve the LP using the Simplex Method and find the optimal value $Z^*$, basic feasible solution, and the inverse basis matrix $B^{-1}$.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
Determine: (i) The range of optimality for $c_1 = 5$ (profit coefficient of concrete blocks). (ii) The shadow price of the second constraint. (iii) The change in $Z^*$ if the second constraint's RHS changes to $b_2 = 8$.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Standard Form with slacks $s_1, s_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } & Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & 6x_1 + 4x_2 + s_1 = 24 \\
& x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 = 6 \\
& \text{all} \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 6 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 24 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 6 |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | 5 | 4 | 0 | 0 | — |

**Iteration 1:** Enter $x_1$ (most positive $c_j - Z_j = 5$). Ratios: $24/6 = 4$, $6/1 = 6$. Pivot row 1.

Performing row operations:

- $R_1 \leftarrow R_1/6$: $(1, 2/3, 1/6, 0, 4)$
- $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - R_1$: $(0, 4/3, -1/6, 1, 2)$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 5 | 1 | 2/3 | 1/6 | 0 | 4 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | 4/3 | $-1/6$ | 1 | 2 |
| $Z_j$ | — | 5 | 10/3 | 5/6 | 0 | 20 |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | 4 - 10/3 = 2/3 | -5/6 | 0 | — |

**Iteration 2:** Enter $x_2$ ($c_j - Z_j = 2/3$). Ratios: $4/(2/3) = 6$, $2/(4/3) = 3/2$. Pivot row 2.

- $R_2 \leftarrow (3/4) R_2$: $(0, 1, -1/8, 3/4, 3/2)$
- $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - (2/3) R_2$: $(1, 0, 1/6 + 1/12, -1/2, 4 - 1) = (1, 0, 1/4, -1/2, 3)$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:

$R_1$ has $x_2$ coefficient $2/3$. To eliminate, $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - (2/3) R_2^{\text{new}}$:

- $R_1: (1 - 0, 2/3 - (2/3)(1), 1/6 - (2/3)(-1/8), 0 - (2/3)(3/4), 4 - (2/3)(3/2))$
- $= (1, 0, 1/6 + 1/12, -1/2, 4 - 1) = (1, 0, 3/12, -1/2, 3) = (1, 0, 1/4, -1/2, 3)$

**Final Optimal Tableau:**

| Basis | $c_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 5 | 1 | 0 | 1/4 | $-1/2$ | 3 |
| $x_2$ | 4 | 0 | 1 | $-1/8$ | 3/4 | 3/2 |
| $Z_j$ | — | 5 | 4 | 9/8 | 1/2 | $Z^* = 21$ |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | — | 0 | 0 | $-9/8$ | $-1/2$ | — |

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 3$, $x_2^* = 3/2$, $Z^* = 21$.

**Inverse Basis Matrix:**

$$
B^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/4 & -1/2 \\ -1/8 & 3/4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

#### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Range of Optimality for $c_1$:**

Let $c_1 = 5 + \Delta c_1$. New $c_{B_1} = 5 + \Delta c_1$, $c_{B_2} = 4$.

For non-basic $s_1$: $Z_{s_1} = (5 + \Delta c_1)(1/4) + (4)(-1/8) = (5 + \Delta c_1)/4 - 1/2$.

$$
c_{s_1} - Z_{s_1} = 0 - [(5 + \Delta c_1)/4 - 1/2] = 1/2 - (5 + \Delta c_1)/4 = (2 - 5 - \Delta c_1)/4 = (-3 - \Delta c_1)/4
$$

For optimality: $(-3 - \Delta c_1)/4 \leq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \geq -3$.

For non-basic $s_2$: $Z_{s_2} = (5 + \Delta c_1)(-1/2) + (4)(3/4) = -(5 + \Delta c_1)/2 + 3$.

$$
c_{s_2} - Z_{s_2} = 0 - [-(5 + \Delta c_1)/2 + 3] = (5 + \Delta c_1)/2 - 3
$$

For optimality: $(5 + \Delta c_1)/2 - 3 \leq 0 \implies \Delta c_1 \leq 1$.

**Allowable increase:** $\Delta c_1^+ = 1$ → $c_1^{\max} = 6$.
**Allowable decrease:** $\Delta c_1^- = 3$ → $c_1^{\min} = 2$.

**Range of Optimality:** $2 \leq c_1 \leq 6$.

**(ii) Shadow Price of Constraint 2:**

$$
\mathbf{y}^{\top} = \mathbf{c}_B^{\top} B^{-1} = (5, 4) \begin{pmatrix} 1/4 & -1/2 \\ -1/8 & 3/4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
y_1 = (5)(1/4) + (4)(-1/8) = 5/4 - 1/2 = 3/4 = 0.75
$$

$$
y_2 = (5)(-1/2) + (4)(3/4) = -5/2 + 3 = 1/2 = 0.5
$$

**Shadow price of constraint 2:** $y_2 = 0.5$.

**(iii) Change in $Z^*$ for $b_2 = 8$:**

$\Delta b_2 = 8 - 6 = 2$.

First, check feasibility. New solution:

$$
\mathbf{x}_B^{\text{new}} = B^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} 24 \\ 8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} (1/4)(24) + (-1/2)(8) \\ (-1/8)(24) + (3/4)(8) \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 - 4 \\ -3 + 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Both components positive ✓. The basis is feasible.

**New $Z^*$:**

$$
Z^{\text{new}} = Z^* + y_2 \Delta b_2 = 21 + (0.5)(2) = 22
$$

**Verification:** $Z^{\text{new}} = 5(2) + 4(3) = 10 + 12 = 22$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify the new solution is feasible** before applying the shadow price formula. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Sign errors in $B^{-1}$ extraction** — students often confuse rows and columns of the slack columns. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 3. **Reporting shadow prices without units** (e.g., saying "$y_1 = 1.8$" instead of "$y_1 = ₹1,800$ per unit of resource"). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up "Range of Optimality" with "Range of Feasibility"** — optimality pertains to $c_j$ changes; feasibility pertains to $b_i$ changes. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 5. **Ignoring the 100% Rule directionality** for objective coefficients (must be all increases or all decreases). (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not stating the new $Z$ is an *approximation*** when the basis is unchanged — strictly, the linear estimate is *exact* when the basis is unchanged, but students often misstate this. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sensitivity Analysis**

- ☐ Sensitivity Analysis is the **post-optimality study** of an LP solution.
- ☐ Three parameter types are studied: $c_j$ (objective), $b_i$ (RHS), and structural coefficients.
- ☐ **$B^{-1}$ extraction from the optimal tableau** is the *gateway* to all sensitivity computations.
- ☐ **Shadow Price** $y_i = \mathbf{c}_B^{\top} B^{-1} \mathbf{e}_i$ = marginal $Z$ change per unit $b_i$ change.
- ☐ **Range of Optimality** = bounds on $c_j$ change keeping the *basis optimal* (sign of $c_j - Z_j$ preserved).
- ☐ **Range of Feasibility** = bounds on $b_i$ change keeping the *basis feasible* (new $\mathbf{x}_B \geq 0$).
- ☐ **New $Z$ formula (exact within range):** $Z^{\text{new}} = Z^* + \sum_i y_i \Delta b_i$.
- ☐ **Negative shadow price** on a $\leq$-constraint ⇒ redundant or degenerate resource.
- ☐ **100% Rule for simultaneous changes** — sum of fractional changes must not exceed 1 (100%).
- ☐ **New variable test:** compute reduced cost; if $\leq 0$ (max), current basis remains optimal.
- ☐ **New constraint test:** if current optimum violates it → invoke the **Dual Simplex Method**.
- ☐ **Civil engineering applications:** construction equipment rental, material procurement, project bid risk assessment, resource reallocation during site disruptions.
- ☐ **Always quote units** for shadow prices; **always verify feasibility** of the new basic solution before claiming the basis is unchanged.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Revised Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET528 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES AND OPERATIONAL RESEARCH FOR CIVIL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Revised Simplex Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Revised Simplex Method (RSM)** is a computationally efficient, matrix-based reformulation of the classical Simplex Method introduced by **George Dantzig and William Orchard-Hayes (1953, 1954)**. Instead of generating and operating on the full Simplex tableau of size $(m+1) \times (n+1)$, the RSM stores and updates only the **basis inverse $B^{-1}$** and works with the original problem data through targeted matrix-vector multiplications. Both methods produce **identical** optimal solutions and basic variable sequences; RSM is merely a **computational strategy** for executing the same pivot logic.

The LPP in standard form considered by RSM:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize (or Minimize) } \quad & Z = \mathbf{c}^{T}\mathbf{x} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \geq \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $A$ is an $m \times n$ coefficient matrix, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, and $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. The matrix $A$ is partitioned as $A = [B \mid N]$, where $B$ is the $m \times m$ **basis matrix** of basic columns and $N$ holds the non-basic columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET528 Module 1):** The RSM is positioned after the standard Simplex Method. Examiners expect students to (a) derive the simplex multipliers, (b) compute entering/leaving variables through the reduced-cost and minimum-ratio logic in matrix form, and (c) execute at least one full numerical iteration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Re-routing" View
Imagine driving from your home in Kerala to a remote construction site. The full Simplex tableau is like printing **every possible route map of India** in front of you — the entire tableau including cities you'll never visit. The Revised Simplex Method is like using a **GPS** that only loads the **next few intersections** and re-calculates them as you progress. It remembers just the **basis inverse (the active route segment)** and queries the **original database (the original constraint coefficients)** only when needed. The result is the same destination (optimum), but you carry a much smaller notebook and make fewer arithmetic operations — a critical advantage in large-scale problems with thousands of variables.

### 1.3 Why RSM Matters for Civil Engineering
Structural optimization (minimum-weight truss design), transportation-network planning (route allocation in highway projects), water-resource distribution (canal/pipe networks), and project-resource scheduling all generate large sparse LPPs. Civil engineering problems typically have **$n \gg m$** (e.g., many candidate road links but few intersection nodes). RSM is the natural tool here because updating a small $m \times m$ inverse is much cheaper than refreshing an $m \times n$ tableau.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention:** Throughout this module, the **basis size $m$** equals the number of equality constraints (after introducing slacks/surplus/artificials), and **$n$** is the total number of decision variables in the final standard form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation
The RSM rests on partitioning the standard-form LPP and exploiting the structure of basic feasible solutions.

**Step 1 — Standard Form & Basis Partitioning.** Convert the LPP to standard form by appending slack, surplus, and artificial variables so that all constraints become equalities with a non-negativity requirement. Identify an initial basic feasible solution (often the slack variables, giving $B = I$). At any iteration, partition $A = [B \mid N]$ and $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_B \\ \mathbf{x}_N \end{bmatrix}$. Since $\mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{0}$ at the current BFS, the constraint equation reduces to $B \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b}$, so $\mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

**Step 2 — The Why: Simplex Multipliers.** The objective function for a basic solution is $Z = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1} \mathbf{b}$. The row vector $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1}$ is the vector of **simplex multipliers** (also called the **dual variables** or **shadow prices**). These multipliers have a profound economic meaning: $\pi_i$ measures the rate at which the objective improves per unit increase in the $i$-th RHS constant $b_i$, holding everything else constant.

**Step 3 — Reduced Costs (Optimality Test).** For every non-basic variable $x_j$ with associated column $\mathbf{a}_j$ of $A$, define the **reduced cost** (also called **relative cost** or **relative profit coefficient**):
$$
\bar{c}_j \;=\; c_j \;-\; \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_j \;=\; c_j \;-\; \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j.
$$
For a **maximization** problem, the current BFS is **optimal** iff $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$ for all non-basic $j$. The entering variable is the one with the **most positive** $\bar{c}_j$. For a **minimization** problem, the optimality condition is $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic $j$, and the entering variable has the **most negative** $\bar{c}_j$.

**Step 4 — Direction Vector (Pricing-Out Test).** If a non-basic variable $x_s$ is selected to enter the basis, increasing it from 0 to 1 forces the basic variables to change along the **direction vector** $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s$ (where $\mathbf{a}_s$ is the $s$-th column of $A$). The feasibility of moving in this direction requires $\mathbf{d}_B \geq \mathbf{0}$ component-wise for at least one leaving variable to be bounded.

**Step 5 — Minimum Ratio Test (Leaving Variable).** The maximum step length before a basic variable becomes negative is
$$
\theta^{*} \;=\; \min_{i \,:\, d_{B,i} > 0} \left\{ \frac{x_{B,i}}{d_{B,i}} \right\}.
$$
The basic variable achieving this minimum **leaves** the basis. If **all** $d_{B,i} \leq 0$, the problem is **unbounded** along the chosen direction.

**Step 6 — Basis Update (How the Inverse Refreshes).** The new basis $B_{\text{new}}$ differs from $B$ only by replacing the leaving column (row $r$ of $B$) with the entering column $\mathbf{a}_s$. The new inverse is
$$
B_{\text{new}}^{-1} \;=\; E_r \, B_{\text{old}}^{-1},
$$
where the **eta matrix** $E_r$ is identity except for the $r$-th column, which is
$$
\boldsymbol{\eta} \;=\; \left( -\frac{d_{B,1}}{d_{B,r}},\; -\frac{d_{B,2}}{d_{B,r}},\; \dots,\; \frac{1}{d_{B,r}},\; \dots,\; -\frac{d_{B,m}}{d_{B,r}} \right)^{T}.
$$
This is the **Product Form of the Inverse (PFI)**. By storing a **list of eta vectors** rather than a fully expanded $B^{-1}$, PFI is extremely memory-efficient for huge sparse problems.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Matrix/Vector Formula** | **Geometric / Economic Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Basic solution | $\mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Current vertex of the feasible polytope |
| Objective value | $Z = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ | Height of supporting hyperplane at current vertex |
| Simplex multipliers | $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1}$ | Dual variables / shadow prices per unit of RHS |
| Reduced cost (any $j$) | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_j$ | Net gain (max) or net loss (min) per unit of $x_j$ |
| Optimality (max) | $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$ for all non-basic $j$ | No improving direction exists |
| Optimality (min) | $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic $j$ | No improving direction exists |
| Direction vector | $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s$ | Movement of basic vars if $x_s$ enters with unit step |
| Step length | $\theta^{*} = \min\limits_{i : d_{B,i} > 0} \left( \dfrac{x_{B,i}}{d_{B,i}} \right)$ | Largest feasible unit step along $\mathbf{d}_B$ |
| Unboundedness | $d_{B,i} \leq 0 \; \forall i$ | Objective can grow without bound |
| Inverse update | $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r \, B_{\text{old}}^{-1}$ | PFI — incremental basis refresh |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory & Speed Advantage:** RSM stores and updates $B^{-1}$ (an $m \times m$ matrix) instead of refreshing the full $m \times n$ tableau. For a problem with $m = 50$ constraints and $n = 5000$ variables, the RSM is roughly **100× faster per iteration** than the tableau method on a workstation with standard BLAS routines, because it avoids $O(mn)$ operations and uses dense $m \times m$ factorizations.

### 2.3 Real-World Civil Engineering Utility
- **Minimum-Weight Truss Design:** RSM solves the LP relaxation of mixed-integer structural optimization with thousands of bar cross-sections.
- **Highway Network Flow:** A road network with thousands of links and a few origin-destination pairs becomes a large sparse LP; RSM with LU factorizations of $B$ is the industry standard.
- **Water Distribution:** Allocating canal/pipe flows to satisfy demand at minimum pumping cost is a typical LP that RSM solves efficiently.
- **Project Resource Allocation:** Multi-project, multi-resource scheduling problems in construction management reduce to large LPs handled routinely by RSM-based solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, HiGHS).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Why $B^{-1}$ Suffices — A Complete Derivation

**Given.** LPP in standard form with a non-singular basis $B$ of size $m \times m$. The cost and variable vectors are partitioned as
$$
\mathbf{c}^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{c}_B^{T} & \mathbf{c}_N^{T} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_B \\ \mathbf{x}_N \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Setting $\mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{0}$ (basic feasible solution), the constraint becomes
$$
B \mathbf{x}_B + N \mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{b} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; B \mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}.
$$
The objective value is therefore
$$
Z = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} \mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{c}_N^{T} \mathbf{x}_N = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1} \mathbf{b}.
$$

**Reduced Cost Derivation.** If $x_j$ (non-basic) is increased by 1 unit, then because of the column $\mathbf{a}_j$, the basic variables must change to maintain $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$. Writing $A\mathbf{x} = B\mathbf{x}_B + \mathbf{a}_j x_j + \sum_{k \neq j,\, k \text{ non-basic}} \mathbf{a}_k x_k = \mathbf{b}$ with all non-basic (other than $x_j$) held at zero,
$$
B\mathbf{x}_B = \mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}_j x_j \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{x}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{b} - B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j x_j = \mathbf{x}_B^{0} - \mathbf{d}_B \, x_j,
$$
where $\mathbf{x}_B^{0} = B^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ is the current BFS and $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_j$. The new objective value is
$$
Z = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} \mathbf{x}_B + c_j x_j = \mathbf{c}_B^{T}\mathbf{x}_B^{0} - \mathbf{c}_B^{T} \mathbf{d}_B \, x_j + c_j x_j.
$$
The **change in $Z$ per unit of $x_j$** is therefore
$$
\Delta Z / \Delta x_j = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{T} \mathbf{d}_B = c_j - \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1} \mathbf{a}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} \mathbf{a}_j = \bar{c}_j.
$$
This rigorously proves the **optimality test**: if $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$ for all non-basic $j$ (max), no improving direction exists. The entering variable is the one with the **largest positive** $\bar{c}_j$ (max case).

**Eta Matrix Derivation.** Let the leaving variable correspond to row $r$ of $B$ and the entering column be $\mathbf{a}_s$. The new basis $B_{\text{new}}$ equals $B$ except that column $r$ is replaced by $\mathbf{a}_s$. We seek a matrix $E_r$ such that $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r B^{-1}$, equivalently $E_r B^{-1} B = E_r = B_{\text{new}}^{-1} B_{\text{new}}$. Choose $E_r$ to be the identity matrix except in column $r$. Since $B_{\text{new}} = B - (\mathbf{b}_r - \mathbf{a}_s)\mathbf{e}_r^{T}$ (where $\mathbf{b}_r$ is the $r$-th column of $B$ and $\mathbf{e}_r$ is the $r$-th unit vector), the Sherman-Morrison rank-one update yields
$$
E_r = I + \left( \boldsymbol{\eta} - \mathbf{e}_r \right) \mathbf{e}_r^{T},
$$
where the eta vector is
$$
\boldsymbol{\eta} \;=\; \left( -\frac{d_{B,1}}{d_{B,r}},\; -\frac{d_{B,2}}{d_{B,r}},\; \dots,\; \frac{1}{d_{B,r}},\; \dots,\; -\frac{d_{B,m}}{d_{B,r}} \right)^{T}.
$$
This compact form is what makes PFI practical.

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example

**Problem.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } \quad Z &= 3x_1 + 2x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2 + 0s_3 \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & 2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 18 \\
& 2x_1 + 3x_2 + s_2 = 42 \\
& 3x_1 + x_2 + s_3 = 24 \\
& x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2, s_3 \geq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$
In matrix form: $A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2 & 3 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{c}^{T} = (3, 2, 0, 0, 0)$.

**Iteration 0 — Initial Basis $[s_1, s_2, s_3]$.**
$$
B_0 = I_{3 \times 3}, \qquad B_0^{-1} = I_{3 \times 3}, \qquad \mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (0, 0, 0).
$$
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B_0^{-1} = (0, 0, 0), \qquad \mathbf{x}_B = B_0^{-1} \mathbf{b} = (18, 42, 24)^{T}.
$$
Reduced costs (using $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_j$):
$$
\bar{c}_{x_1} = 3 - 0 = 3, \quad \bar{c}_{x_2} = 2 - 0 = 2, \quad \bar{c}_{s_1}=\bar{c}_{s_2}=\bar{c}_{s_3}=0.
$$
Entering: $x_1$ (largest positive $\bar{c}_j$). $\mathbf{a}_s = (2, 2, 3)^{T}$.

Direction vector:
$$
\mathbf{d}_B = B_0^{-1} \mathbf{a}_s = (2, 2, 3)^{T}.
$$
Minimum ratio test:
$$
\theta^{*} = \min\!\left( \frac{18}{2},\; \frac{42}{2},\; \frac{24}{3} \right) = \min(9,\, 21,\, 8) = 8 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{leaving variable} = s_3 \text{ (row } r=3\text{)}.
$$
Eta vector:
$$
\boldsymbol{\eta} = \left( -\frac{2}{3},\; -\frac{2}{3},\; \frac{1}{3} \right)^{T}.
$$

**Iteration 1 — New Basis $[s_1, s_2, x_1]$.**
$$
B_1^{-1} = E_3 \, B_0^{-1} = E_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
(Note: many texts absorb the 1/$d_{B,r}$ factor into the row scale; here we keep the standard form.)
Updated BFS:
$$
\mathbf{x}_B = B_1^{-1} \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 - 16 \\ 42 - 16 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 26 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $s_1 = 2$, $s_2 = 26$, $x_1 = 8$ (with $x_2 = s_3 = 0$).

Updated multipliers: $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (0, 0, 3)$.
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (0, 0, 3) \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix} = \left( 0,\; 0,\; 1 \right).
$$
Reduced costs of non-basic variables:
$$
\bar{c}_{x_2} = 2 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{x_2} = 2 - (0, 0, 1)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 2 - 1 = 1,
$$
$$
\bar{c}_{s_3} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{s_3} = 0 - (0, 0, 1)\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = -1.
$$
Since $\bar{c}_{x_2} = 1 > 0$, the current solution is **not optimal**. Entering: $x_2$, $\mathbf{a}_s = (1, 3, 1)^{T}$.
$$
\mathbf{d}_B = B_1^{-1} \mathbf{a}_s = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 2/3 \\ 3 - 2/3 \\ 1/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 \\ 7/3 \\ 1/3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Minimum ratio test:
$$
\theta^{*} = \min\!\left( \frac{2}{1/3},\; \frac{26}{7/3},\; \frac{8}{1/3} \right) = \min(6,\; 78/7,\; 24) = \min(6,\; 11.14,\; 24) = 6.
$$
Leaving variable: $s_1$ (row $r = 1$).

Eta vector for this pivot:
$$
\boldsymbol{\eta} = \left( \frac{1}{1/3},\; -\frac{7/3}{1/3},\; -\frac{1/3}{1/3} \right)^{T} = \left( 3,\; -7,\; -1 \right)^{T}.
$$

**Iteration 2 — New Basis $[x_2, s_2, x_1]$.**
$$
B_2^{-1} = E_1 B_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & 0 \\ -7 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 1 & -2/3 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ -7 & 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Updated BFS:
$$
\mathbf{x}_B = B_2^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ -7 & 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 54 - 48 \\ -126 + 42 + 96 \\ -18 + 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 12 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $x_2 = 6$, $s_2 = 12$, $x_1 = 6$, with $s_1 = s_3 = 0$.

Updated multipliers: $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (2, 0, 3)$.
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (2, 0, 3) \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ -7 & 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \left( 6 - 0 - 3,\; 0 + 0 + 0,\; -4 + 0 + 3 \right) = (3,\; 0,\; -1).
$$
Reduced costs of remaining non-basic variables ($s_1$ and $s_3$):
$$
\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{s_1} = 0 - (3, 0, -1)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -3,
$$
$$
\bar{c}_{s_3} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{s_3} = 0 - (3, 0, -1)\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = +1.
$$
Since we are maximizing, we need all $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$. Here $\bar{c}_{s_3} = 1 > 0$, so we must continue. **Entering: $s_3$**, $\mathbf{a}_s = (0, 0, 1)^{T}$.
$$
\mathbf{d}_B = B_2^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ -7 & 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Ratio test (only $d_{B,i} > 0$ considered):
$$
\theta^{*} = \min\!\left( \frac{12}{4},\; \frac{6}{1} \right) = \min(3,\; 6) = 3.
$$
Leaving variable: $s_2$ (row $r=2$).

Eta vector:
$$
\boldsymbol{\eta} = \left( \frac{2}{4},\; \frac{1}{4},\; -\frac{1}{4} \right)^{T} = \left( \tfrac{1}{2},\; \tfrac{1}{4},\; -\tfrac{1}{4} \right)^{T}.
$$

**Iteration 3 — New Basis $[x_2, s_3, x_1]$.**
$$
B_3^{-1} = E_2 B_2^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1/2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/4 & 0 \\ 0 & -1/4 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 0 & -2 \\ -7 & 1 & 4 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\ -7/4 & 1/4 & 1 \\ 3/4 & -1/4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
Updated BFS:
$$
\mathbf{x}_B = B_3^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\ -7/4 & 1/4 & 1 \\ 3/4 & -1/4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 24 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -9 + 21 \\ -63/2 + 21/2 + 24 \\ 27/2 - 21/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 \\ 3 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$
So $x_2 = 12$, $s_3 = 3$, $x_1 = 3$.

Multipliers: $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (2, 0, 3)$.
$$
\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (2, 0, 3) \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 & 1/2 & 0 \\ -7/4 & 1/4 & 1 \\ 3/4 & -1/4 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = (-1 + 0 + 9/4,\; 1 + 0 - 3/4,\; 0 + 0 + 0) = (5/4,\; 1/4,\; 0).
$$
Reduced costs of $s_1$ and $s_2$:
$$
\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{s_1} = 0 - (5/4, 1/4, 0)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -5/4,
$$
$$
\bar{c}_{s_2} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}\mathbf{a}_{s_2} = 0 - (5/4, 1/4, 0)\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = -1/4.
$$
Both $\bar{c}_{s_1}, \bar{c}_{s_2} < 0$, so the current BFS is **optimal**.

**Final Optimal Solution.**
$$
\boxed{\,x_1 = 3,\; x_2 = 12,\; s_1 = 0,\; s_2 = 0,\; s_3 = 3,\; Z^{*} = 3(3) + 2(12) = 33.\,}
$$
Verification: $2(3) + 1(12) = 18$ ✓; $2(3) + 3(12) = 42$ ✓; $3(3) + 1(12) = 21 \leq 24$ ✓ (slack $s_3 = 3$). All variables non-negative. Maximum $Z = 33$ confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Iteration Count:** The full tableau method yields the same optimum in the same number of pivots, but RSM required only the inversion of small $3 \times 3$ matrices and a few matrix-vector products — operations that scale linearly rather than quadratically with the number of variables.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (RSM Iteration Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START: LPP in standard form]) --> initB[Identify initial basis B and cB]
    initB --> compInv[Compute or update B-inverse]
    compInv --> compPi[Compute simplex multipliers pi = cB-transpose times B-inverse]
    compPi --> compXb[Compute current BFS: xB = B-inverse times b]
    compXb --> compRc[Compute reduced costs c-bar-j for all non-basic j]
    compRc --> optTest{All c-bar-j le 0 for MAX?}
    optTest -- YES --> stop([STOP: Optimal solution found])
    optTest -- NO --> enterVar[Select entering variable x-s with most positive c-bar-j]
    enterVar --> compDir[Compute direction vector dB = B-inverse times a-s]
    compDir --> dirTest{All dB-i le 0?}
    dirTest -- YES --> unbd([STOP: Problem is UNBOUNDED])
    dirTest -- NO --> minRatio[Minimum ratio test: theta-star = min xB-i over dB-i with dB-i greater than 0]
    minRatio --> leaveVar[Identify leaving variable from min ratio row r]
    leaveVar --> buildEta[Form eta matrix E-r with pivot column eta]
    buildEta --> updInv[Update B-inverse: B-new-inverse = E-r times B-old-inverse]
    updInv --> compPi
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Module]
        A[Original A matrix and b vector and c vector]
    end
    subgraph BASIS[Basis Management Module]
        B1[Current Basis B]
        B2[B-inverse Storage]
        B3[Eta Vector List for PFI]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Module]
        C1[pi = cB-transpose B-inverse]
        C2[xB = B-inverse b]
        C3[c-bar-j = cj minus pi-transpose a-j]
        C4[dB = B-inverse a-s]
        C5[theta-star = min ratio test]
    end
    subgraph DECIDE[Decision Module]
        D1{Optimal?}
        D2{Unbounded?}
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Module]
        O1[Optimal x and Z]
    end
    A --> BASIS
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C4
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 -- NO --> C4
    C4 --> D2
    D2 -- NO --> C5
    C5 --> B3
    B3 --> B2
    D1 -- YES --> O1
```

### 4.3 Information Flow in One RSM Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DATA[Original Problem Data - read-only]
        A1[Column a-s of A]
        A2[Vector c]
        A3[Vector b]
    end
    subgraph STATE[Current State]
        S1[B-inverse]
        S2[cB vector]
        S3[xB vector]
    end
    subgraph CALC[Calculations This Iteration]
        K1[pi = cB-transpose B-inverse]
        K2[c-bar-s = c-s minus pi-transpose a-s]
        K3[dB = B-inverse a-s]
        K4[theta-star = min over positive dB-i of xB-i over dB-i]
    end
    subgraph NEXT[Next Iteration Updates]
        N1[New B-inverse via Eta]
        N2[New cB]
        N3[New xB]
    end
    S1 --> K1
    S2 --> K1
    K1 --> K2
    A2 --> K2
    A1 --> K3
    S1 --> K3
    S3 --> K4
    K3 --> K4
    K2 --> nextCheck{Optimal?}
    nextCheck -- NO --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    K4 --> N1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]** Define the **Revised Simplex Method**. How does it differ from the standard Simplex tableau method?

**Model Answer:**
The Revised Simplex Method (RSM) is a matrix-algebraic reformulation of the classical Simplex Method that, instead of operating on the full $m \times (n+1)$ tableau, stores and updates only the **basis inverse $B^{-1}$** and computes simplex multipliers, reduced costs, and direction vectors through targeted matrix-vector products.
**Key differences:**
1. **Storage:** RSM stores an $m \times m$ matrix $B^{-1}$ and a list of eta vectors; tableau method stores the entire $m \times (n+1)$ tableau.
2. **Computation per iteration:** RSM uses $O(m^2)$ matrix-vector products; tableau method uses $O(mn)$ row operations.
3. **Numerical accuracy:** RSM postpones round-off accumulation by working with the original $A$, $\mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{c}$ and only refreshing $B^{-1}$ incrementally via the PFI.
4. **Sparse exploitation:** RSM preserves sparsity of $A$ because it only multiplies $B^{-1}$ by individual columns $\mathbf{a}_j$, not the full $A$.
5. **Output:** Both methods yield the same optimal $\mathbf{x}^{*}$, $Z^{*}$, and dual prices $\boldsymbol{\pi}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar]** What are **simplex multipliers**? State their economic interpretation.

**Model Answer:**
The simplex multipliers are the row vector $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1}$ associated with the current basis $B$. They are also called the **dual variables** or **shadow prices**.
**Economic interpretation:** The $i$-th component $\pi_i$ represents the **rate of change of the optimal objective value $Z^{*}$ per unit increase in the $i$-th right-hand-side constant $b_i$**, with all other parameters held fixed. In civil-engineering contexts, $\pi_i$ may represent the marginal value of an additional unit of resource (e.g., extra cubic meter of water, extra man-hour of labour) at the optimum, even if that resource is not physically used in the current solution.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as Part (a) — 7 marks, Part (b) — 7 marks. You are expected to attempt **one full question** from the choice offered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the LPP:
$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2
$$
subject to
$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8, \qquad x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 9, \qquad x_1, x_2 \geq 0.
$$
Convert to standard form and **solve the first two iterations of the Revised Simplex Method**. Clearly state the simplex multipliers, reduced costs, entering and leaving variables, and the updated basis inverse in matrix form at each step.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Product Form of the Inverse (PFI)**. Show how the eta matrix $E_r$ is constructed from the direction vector $\mathbf{d}_B$, and prove that $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r B_{\text{old}}^{-1}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Step 1 — Standard form.* Introduce slacks $s_1, s_2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 8 \\
x_1 + 3x_2 + s_2 &= 9 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 &\geq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$
$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = (8, 9)^{T}$, $\mathbf{c}^{T} = (4, 3, 0, 0)$.

*Iteration 0 — Basis $[s_1, s_2]$, $B_0 = I$.*
$B_0^{-1} = I$, $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (0, 0)$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (0, 0)$.
$\mathbf{x}_B = (8, 9)^{T}$.
$\bar{c}_{x_1} = 4$, $\bar{c}_{x_2} = 3$. Enter $x_1$ ($\bar{c}_{x_1} > \bar{c}_{x_2}$). $\mathbf{a}_s = (2, 1)^{T}$.
$\mathbf{d}_B = B_0^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s = (2, 1)^{T}$.
$\theta^{*} = \min(8/2, 9/1) = \min(4, 9) = 4$. Leave $s_1$ (row $r=1$).
Eta vector: $\boldsymbol{\eta} = (1/2, -1/2)^{T}$, so $E_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ -1/2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

*Iteration 1 — Basis $[x_1, s_2]$.*
$B_1^{-1} = E_1 B_0^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/2 & 0 \\ -1/2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
$\mathbf{x}_B = B_1^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (4, 5)^{T}$. So $x_1 = 4$, $s_2 = 5$.
$\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (4, 0)$, $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (4, 0) B_1^{-1} = (2, 0)$.
$\bar{c}_{x_2} = 3 - (2, 0)(1, 3)^{T} = 3 - 2 = 1 > 0$. **Not optimal.** Enter $x_2$, $\mathbf{a}_s = (1, 3)^{T}$.
$\mathbf{d}_B = B_1^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s = (1/2, 5/2)^{T}$.
$\theta^{*} = \min(4/(1/2), 5/(5/2)) = \min(8, 2) = 2$. Leave $s_2$ (row $r=2$).
Eta vector: $\boldsymbol{\eta} = (-1/5, 2/5)^{T}$, $E_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1/5 \\ 0 & 2/5 \end{bmatrix}$.

*Iteration 2 (informational).* $B_2^{-1} = E_2 B_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 3/5 & -1/5 \\ -1/5 & 2/5 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{x}_B = B_2^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (3, 1)^{T}$. $\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}(1,0)^{T} \leq 0$, $\bar{c}_{s_2} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}(0,1)^{T} \leq 0$. **Optimal:** $x_1 = 3$, $x_2 = 1$, $Z^{*} = 15$.

**(b)** *PFI Construction.* The Product Form of the Inverse represents $B^{-1}$ at any iteration as a product of eta matrices, $B^{-1} = E_k E_{k-1} \cdots E_1 B_0^{-1}$, where each $E_t$ corresponds to one pivot. The eta matrix for a pivot on row $r$ with direction $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s$ is
$$
E_r = I + (\boldsymbol{\eta} - \mathbf{e}_r)\mathbf{e}_r^{T},
$$
where
$$
\eta_i = \begin{cases} -\dfrac{d_{B,i}}{d_{B,r}}, & i \neq r, \\[4pt] \dfrac{1}{d_{B,r}}, & i = r. \end{cases}
$$
*Proof of $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r B_{\text{old}}^{-1}$:* Let $B_{\text{new}} = B_{\text{old}} - (\mathbf{b}_r - \mathbf{a}_s)\mathbf{e}_r^{T}$, where $\mathbf{b}_r$ is the $r$-th column of $B_{\text{old}}$. Substituting $\mathbf{a}_s = B_{\text{old}} \mathbf{d}_B$ (definition of direction vector) and applying the Sherman-Morrison rank-one formula gives $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r B_{\text{old}}^{-1}$ with $E_r$ as above. Hence the new inverse is obtained by **pre-multiplying** the old inverse by a single $m \times m$ eta matrix. Repeating across iterations yields the **product form** $B^{-1} = E_k E_{k-1} \cdots E_1 B_0^{-1}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the LPP:
$$
\text{Minimize } Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2
$$
subject to
$$
6x_1 + 4x_2 \geq 24, \qquad x_1 + 2x_2 \geq 6, \qquad x_1, x_2 \geq 0.
$$
Use the **Revised Simplex Method** (with Big-M if necessary) to obtain the **optimal solution**. State the optimal $Z$ value, the basic and non-basic variables, and the simplex multipliers at the optimum.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **Revised Simplex Method** with the **standard Simplex Method** in terms of (i) computational effort, (ii) memory, (iii) numerical stability, (iv) handling of bounded variables, and (v) suitability for sensitivity analysis.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Standard form with surplus and artificials (Big-M).* Subtract surpluses $s_1, s_2$ and add artificials $a_1, a_2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
6x_1 + 4x_2 - s_1 + a_1 &= 24 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 - s_2 + a_2 &= 6 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2, a_1, a_2 &\geq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$
Minimize $Z = 5x_1 + 4x_2 + 0 s_1 + 0 s_2 + M a_1 + M a_2$. Initial basis $B_0 = [a_1, a_2] = I$, $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (M, M)$. $B_0^{-1} = I$.
$\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (M, M)$. $\mathbf{x}_B = (24, 6)^{T}$.
$\bar{c}_{x_1} = 5 - (M, M)(6, 1)^{T} = 5 - 7M$ (very negative).
$\bar{c}_{x_2} = 4 - (M, M)(4, 2)^{T} = 4 - 6M$ (very negative).
For minimization, the entering variable is the most negative $\bar{c}_j$. **Enter $x_1$** (more negative). $\mathbf{a}_s = (6, 1)^{T}$.
$\mathbf{d}_B = (6, 1)^{T}$. $\theta^{*} = \min(24/6, 6/1) = \min(4, 6) = 4$. Leave $a_1$.
$\boldsymbol{\eta} = (1/6, -1/6)^{T}$.

*Iteration 1 — Basis $[x_1, a_2]$.*
$B_1^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 1/6 & 0 \\ -1/6 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. $\mathbf{x}_B = (4, 2)^{T}$. $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (5, M)$.
$\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (5, M) B_1^{-1} = (5/6 - M/6,\; M)$.
$\bar{c}_{x_2} = 4 - (5/6 - M/6, M)(4, 2)^{T} = 4 - 20/6 + 2M/3 - 2M = 4 - 10/3 - 4M/3 = 2/3 - 4M/3$.
$\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}(-1, 0)^{T} = 5/6 - M/6$.
$\bar{c}_{s_2} = 0 - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}(0, -1)^{T} = M$ (positive — not entering in min).
Most negative: $\bar{c}_{x_2}$ if $M$ large. **Enter $x_2$**. $\mathbf{a}_s = (4, 2)^{T}$.
$\mathbf{d}_B = B_1^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s = (2/3, 1/3)^{T}$. $\theta^{*} = \min(4/(2/3), 2/(1/3)) = \min(6, 6) = 6$. Tie: either $a_2$ or $x_1$ may leave; choose $a_2$ (row 2).
$\boldsymbol{\eta} = (-1, 3)^{T}$ (or scaled form).

*Iteration 2 — Basis $[x_1, x_2]$.*
$\mathbf{x}_B = (3, 3)^{T}$ after the pivot. $\mathbf{c}_B^{T} = (5, 4)$. $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B_2^{-1}$. Reduced costs of $s_1, s_2, a_1, a_2$ are all $\geq 0$ (min case) once $M$ is large.
**Optimal:** $x_1 = 3$, $x_2 = 3$, $Z^{*} = 5(3) + 4(3) = 27$. Simplex multipliers: $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = (5/6, 7/6)$ (the dual values).

**(b)** *Comparison:*

| **Criterion** | **Standard Tableau Simplex** | **Revised Simplex Method** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Computational effort | $O(mn)$ per iteration (full row ops) | $O(m^2)$ per iteration (small $B^{-1}$ updates) |
| (ii) Memory | $O(mn)$ for full tableau | $O(m^2 + p)$ where $p$ = number of eta vectors |
| (iii) Numerical stability | More round-off accumulation | Original data preserved; round-off smaller |
| (iv) Bounded variables | Awkward row additions | Easier via column augmentation or variable split |
| (v) Sensitivity analysis | Re-run from scratch needed | Reuse stored $B^{-1}$ for RHS/cost changes; cheaper |

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Points for RSM Questions:**
> 1. **Sign error in reduced-cost optimality condition:** Students often write "enter if $\bar{c}_j > 0$" for minimization, which is wrong. For **min**, enter the most **negative** $\bar{c}_j$ and stop when all $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting to recompute $\mathbf{c}_B$** after a basis change before computing $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T}$. The basis matrix $B$ changes, so $\mathbf{c}_B$ must change with it.
> 3. **Confusing rows and columns in the eta matrix.** The eta vector replaces column $r$ of $I$, where $r$ is the **row index** of the leaving variable in the BFS (not the variable number).
> 4. **Skipping the unit-step direction check.** If $d_{B,i} \leq 0$ for all $i$, the LP is **unbounded** — examiners give 0 marks for missing this case.
> 5. **Not stating initial BFS explicitly.** For a problem with artificials (Phase I or Big-M), students must verify that the initial BFS is feasible (e.g., $B_0^{-1}\mathbf{b} \geq 0$).
> 6. **Mixing-up $\mathbf{a}_s$ (column of $A$) with row.** The direction vector is $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s$, where $\mathbf{a}_s$ is the **column** of $A$ corresponding to the entering variable.
> 7. **Forgetting to update $B^{-1}$ via the eta matrix at every iteration.** Examiners check that the new $\mathbf{x}_B$ matches $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **RSM = Simplex Method using $B^{-1}$ only**, not the full tableau.
- [x] **Initial basis** is the identity matrix of slacks (after standard-form conversion).
- [x] **Simplex multipliers** $\boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} = \mathbf{c}_B^{T} B^{-1}$ are dual variables / shadow prices.
- [x] **Reduced cost** $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \boldsymbol{\pi}^{T} \mathbf{a}_j$ is the net improvement per unit of $x_j$.
- [x] **Optimality (max):** All $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$ for non-basic $j$.
- [x] **Optimality (min):** All $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$ for non-basic $j$.
- [x] **Entering variable:** Largest positive $\bar{c}_j$ (max) or largest negative $\bar{c}_j$ (min).
- [x] **Direction vector:** $\mathbf{d}_B = B^{-1}\mathbf{a}_s$.
- [x] **Unboundedness test:** If $\mathbf{d}_B \leq \mathbf{0}$, the LP is unbounded along that direction.
- [x] **Leaving variable:** Row achieving $\theta^{*} = \min\limits_{d_{B,i} > 0} x_{B,i}/d_{B,i}$.
- [x] **Eta vector** $\eta_i = -d_{B,i}/d_{B,r}$ for $i \neq r$, $\eta_r = 1/d_{B,r}$.
- [x] **Inverse update:** $B_{\text{new}}^{-1} = E_r B_{\text{old}}^{-1}$ (PFI).
- [x] **Computational advantage:** RSM is $O(m^2)$ vs. tableau's $O(mn)$ per iteration.
- [x] **Memory advantage:** Only $m \times m$ data and eta-vector list needed.
- [x] **Sensitivity advantage:** Stored $B^{-1}$ makes RHS/cost re-optimization fast.
- [x] **Civil-engineering relevance:** Truss design, transportation, water resources, project scheduling — all yield large sparse LPs ideal for RSM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation and Assignment Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET528 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES AND OPERATIONAL RESEARCH FOR CIVIL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transportation and Assignment Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Transportation Problem (TP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Transportation Problem** is a special class of Linear Programming Problem (LPP) that deals with the distribution of a **homogeneous product** from a set of *m* origins (sources) to a set of *n* destinations (sinks), subject to supply constraints at origins and demand constraints at destinations, with the objective of minimizing the total transportation cost.

**Mathematical Formulation (Standard Form):**

Let:
- $x_{ij}$ = quantity transported from source $i$ to destination $j$
- $c_{ij}$ = unit transportation cost from source $i$ to destination $j$
- $a_i$ = supply (capacity) at source $i$
- $b_j$ = demand (requirement) at destination $j$

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$

Subject to:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \quad \text{(Supply constraints)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Demand constraints)}$$

$$x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j$$

**Balanced Condition (Mandatory for Standard Methods):**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j = T$$

When this equality holds, the problem is **balanced**; otherwise, it is **unbalanced** and requires a **dummy row/column** with zero cost.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Cement Warehouse Network"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Civil Engineering Analogy:**
> Imagine you are the logistics head of a cement company in Kerala. You have **3 warehouses (sources)** at Kochi, Calicut, and Thiruvananthapuram with limited cement stock (**supply**). Four major construction sites (**destinations**) at Thrissur, Kottayam, Kannur, and Kollam require specific quantities (**demand**). The cost to ship one tonne from each warehouse to each site differs based on distance, tolls, and route.
>
> The **Transportation Problem** asks: *"Which warehouse should supply which site, and how much, so that ALL demand is met from available supply at the LOWEST total shipping cost?"*

This is solved using a **matrix/tabular format** rather than the simplex method (because the structure is highly degenerate, and a custom solution is far more efficient).

---

### 1.3 Assignment Problem (AP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Assignment Problem** is a special variant of the Transportation Problem where the objective is to assign *n* jobs to *n* machines (or *n* workers to *n* tasks) on a **one-to-one basis** (each job assigned to exactly one machine), such that the total assignment cost (or time) is **minimized** (or **maximized** for profit).

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \, x_{ij}$$

Subject to:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad i = 1, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Each job assigned once)}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad j = 1, \ldots, n \quad \text{(Each machine gets one job)}$$

$$x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$$

**Key Property:** It is a Transportation Problem where $a_i = b_j = 1$ for all $i, j$, so it has exactly $n$ allocations. The structure allows the use of the **Hungarian Method**, which is $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ complexity — far faster than solving the LPP via simplex.

---

### 1.4 Key Assumptions of TP & AP

| Assumption | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Homogeneity** | The product (or job/task) is identical across all sources/destinations |
| **Deterministic costs** | $c_{ij}$ values are known and fixed |
| **Definite supply/demand** | $a_i$ and $b_j$ are fixed constants |
| **Non-negativity** | All $x_{ij} \geq 0$ |
| **Independence** | Cost from source $i$ to destination $j$ is independent of other allocations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert:** Students often forget that **integer values are automatically guaranteed** in TP and AP without explicit integer constraints — this is called the **Integrality Property** of transportation matrices. The simplex tableau of TP/AP always yields integer solutions.

---

### 1.5 Difference Between TP and AP

| Feature | Transportation Problem | Assignment Problem |
|---|---|---|
| Number of allocations | $m + n - 1$ | $n$ (always) |
| Supply/Demand | Variable ($a_i, b_j \geq 1$) | Always equals 1 |
| Solution method | NWCM, LCM, VAM + MODI | Hungarian Method |
| Variables | Many per cell | Only 0 or 1 per cell |
| Computational approach | Matrix + dual variables | Row/column reduction |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stages of Solving a Transportation Problem

The complete solution process has **three sequential stages**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS):**
> Find a starting feasible solution using any one of three heuristics:
> 1. **North-West Corner Method (NWCM)**
> 2. **Least Cost Method (LCM)**
> 3. **Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** — *most accurate IBFS*

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Optimality Test:**
> Verify if the current solution is optimal using one of:
> 1. **Stepping Stone Method**
> 2. **Modified Distribution (MODI) Method / $u-v$ Method** — *preferred for exams*

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Iterative Improvement (if not optimal):**
> Identify the entering variable (most negative $\Delta_{ij}$), form a closed loop, apply stepping-stone adjustments, and repeat Stage 2 until all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$.

---

### 2.2 Methods for IBFS – Comparative Analysis

#### 2.2.1 North-West Corner Method (NWCM)

**Logic:** Start at the top-left cell (north-west). Allocate the minimum of remaining supply and demand. Move right if demand is exhausted; otherwise move down. Ignore cost entirely.

**Advantages:** Simplest, fastest, no cost computation.
**Disadvantages:** Gives the most expensive IBFS — usually far from optimal.

#### 2.2.2 Least Cost Method (LCM)

**Logic:** Among all unallocated cells, pick the cell with the **lowest unit cost** $c_{ij}$. Allocate as much as possible there. Strike off the exhausted row/column and repeat.

**Advantages:** Better IBFS than NWCM.
**Disadvantages:** Still ignores opportunity cost (penalty of NOT using a cell).

#### 2.2.3 Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM) — *Most Preferred*

**Logic:** For each row and column, compute the **penalty** = difference between the two smallest $c_{ij}$ values. Pick the row/column with the **highest penalty**, then allocate the maximum possible to its **lowest-cost cell**.

**Advantages:** Produces IBFS closest to optimal solution.
**Disadvantages:** Computationally heavier (requires penalty recomputation).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question says *"find the IBFS"*, use VAM unless explicitly told otherwise. When a question says *"obtain an initial solution by any method"*, the simplest (NWCM) is acceptable.

---

### 2.3 Optimality Test — MODI (u-v) Method

**Key Idea:** The dual variables $u_i$ (for each row) and $v_j$ (for each column) are assigned to occupied cells such that:

$$u_i + v_j = c_{ij} \quad \text{for every occupied cell } (i, j)$$

For an **unoccupied cell** $(i, j)$, the **opportunity cost** is:

$$\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$$

**Optimality Condition:** The solution is **optimal** if and only if $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ for all unoccupied cells.

If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, the cell with the **most negative** $\Delta_{ij}$ enters the basis, and the **closed-loop stepping-stone** method is used to update the solution.

**Setting the Reference:** Since there are $m + n - 1$ occupied cells and $m + n$ unknowns, set any one $u_i$ or $v_j$ = 0 (typically $u_1 = 0$), then back-solve.

---

### 2.4 Degeneracy in Transportation Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A TP solution is **degenerate** if the number of occupied cells is **less than** $m + n - 1$.

**Cause:** Occurs when allocations exhaust a row AND column simultaneously during IBFS construction (more than once in the process).

**Resolution:** Introduce **$\epsilon$ (epsilon)** — a tiny positive number (≈ 0) — in one of the previously struck-out rows/columns to artificially fill an empty cell without affecting cost.

$$\boxed{\text{Number of allocations required} = m + n - 1}$$

For an $m \times n$ transportation tableau.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Objective Function (TP) | $\min Z = \sum_{i,j} c_{ij} x_{ij}$ | Linear objective |
| Optimality Check (MODI) | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ | Must satisfy $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ |
| Dual Constraint (occupied) | $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ | Holds for all occupied cells |
| Number of Allocations | $m + n - 1$ | Basis size |
| Degeneracy Indicator | $\text{Allocations} < m + n - 1$ | Add $\epsilon$ cells |
| VAM Penalty | $\text{Penalty} = \vert c_{\text{2nd smallest}} - c_{\text{smallest}} \vert$ | Per row AND per column |
| Assignment Problem Size | $n$ allocations | Always $n$ jobs to $n$ machines |
| Hungarian Reduction | $\min$ from rows, then $\min$ from columns | Both must produce $\geq 1$ zeros |
| Maximization → Minimization | $\text{New } c_{ij} = M - c_{ij}$ | $M$ = largest cost in matrix |
| Unbalanced TP (Supply > Demand) | Add dummy column | Dummy cost = 0, dummy demand = $\sum a_i - \sum b_j$ |
| Unbalanced TP (Demand > Supply) | Add dummy row | Dummy cost = 0, dummy supply = $\sum b_j - \sum a_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MODI is preferred over Stepping Stone in exams:**
> MODI requires only **simple addition/subtraction** once $u_i, v_j$ are known, whereas Stepping Stone requires manual loop tracing for every unoccupied cell — extremely tedious and error-prone for a 3×4 or larger matrix.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Construction Logistics** | Optimizing cement/steel/aggregate dispatch from plants to sites |
| **Project Scheduling** | Allocating crews to tasks minimizing total man-hours |
| **Waste Management** | Routing construction debris from sites to recycling yards |
| **Water Distribution** | Pumping water from reservoirs to demand zones |
| **Equipment Deployment** | Assigning excavators/cranes to project sites |
| **Survey Teams** | Assigning surveyors to survey zones to minimize travel cost |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Transportation Problem (Complete Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A construction company has **3 plants (A, B, C)** supplying ready-mix concrete to **4 sites (1, 2, 3, 4)**. The unit shipping cost (in ₹ per cubic metre), supply (in $100\text{ m}^3$), and demand (in $100\text{ m}^3$) are given:

| | Site 1 | Site 2 | Site 3 | Site 4 | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plant A** | 4 | 8 | 8 | 6 | **14** |
| **Plant B** | 6 | 3 | 5 | 7 | **12** |
| **Plant C** | 5 | 4 | 6 | 9 | **10** |
| **Demand** | **8** | **10** | **9** | **7** | **36 / 36** |

Total Supply = $14 + 12 + 10 = 36$ = Total Demand = $8 + 10 + 9 + 7 = 36$. **Balanced.**

---

#### STEP 1: Find IBFS using VAM (Vogel's Approximation Method)

**Iteration 1:**

Row penalties (difference between two smallest in each row):
- Row A: $8 - 4 = 4$ (smallest 4, second smallest 6 → wait, sorting: 4, 6, 8, 8 → penalty = 6 − 4 = **2**)
- Row B: sort = 3, 5, 6, 7 → penalty = 5 − 3 = **2**
- Row C: sort = 4, 5, 6, 9 → penalty = 5 − 4 = **1**

Column penalties:
- Col 1: 4, 5, 6 → 5 − 4 = **1**
- Col 2: 3, 4, 8 → 4 − 3 = **1**
- Col 3: 5, 6, 8 → 6 − 5 = **1**
- Col 4: 6, 7, 9 → 7 − 6 = **1**

**Highest penalty = 2** (tied between Row A and Row B).
- Row A's lowest cost = 4 (Cell A-1). Allocate $\min(14, 8) = 8$.
- Site 1 demand exhausted → strike out Col 1. Plant A supply remaining = 14 − 8 = 6.

**Iteration 2:**

| | Site 2 | Site 3 | Site 4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | 8 | 6 | 6 |
| B | 3 | 5 | 7 | 12 |
| C | 4 | 6 | 9 | 10 |
| Demand | 10 | 9 | 7 | |

Row penalties:
- Row A: 6, 8, 8 → 8 − 6 = **2**
- Row B: 3, 5, 7 → 5 − 3 = **2**
- Row C: 4, 6, 9 → 6 − 4 = **2**

Column penalties:
- Col 2: 3, 4, 8 → 4 − 3 = **1**
- Col 3: 5, 6, 8 → 6 − 5 = **1**
- Col 4: 6, 7, 9 → 7 − 6 = **1**

**Highest penalty = 2** (tied: A, B, C). Pick Row B (lowest cost cell = 3 at B-2). Allocate $\min(12, 10) = 10$.
- Site 2 demand exhausted. Plant B supply = 12 − 10 = 2.

**Iteration 3:**

| | Site 3 | Site 4 | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | 6 | 6 |
| B | 5 | 7 | 2 |
| C | 6 | 9 | 10 |
| Demand | 9 | 7 | |

Row penalties:
- Row A: 6, 8 → 8 − 6 = **2**
- Row B: 5, 7 → 7 − 5 = **2**
- Row C: 6, 9 → 9 − 6 = **3**

Column penalties:
- Col 3: 5, 6, 8 → 6 − 5 = **1**
- Col 4: 6, 7, 9 → 7 − 6 = **1**

**Highest penalty = 3** (Row C). Lowest cost in Row C = 6 (Cell C-3). Allocate $\min(10, 9) = 9$.
- Site 3 demand exhausted. Plant C supply = 10 − 9 = 1.

**Iteration 4:** Remaining rows A, B; remaining column 4. Allocate to remaining cells:
- Cell A-4: $\min(6, 7) = 6$
- Cell B-4: $\min(2, 1) = 1$
- Cell C-4: $\min(1, 0) = 0$ — wait, but C-4 has cost 9. After C-4 allocation: demand at Site 4 = 7 − 6 − 1 = 0. So C-4 demand is also 0? Let's recompute.

After Cell A-4 = 6: Demand Site 4 = 7 − 6 = 1. Plant A exhausted.
After Cell B-4 = $\min(2, 1) = 1$: Demand Site 4 = 1 − 1 = 0. Site 4 done.

Plant C still has supply = 10 − 9 = 1 leftover, but no demand left. This is impossible — means the IBFS has a problem. Let me recheck.

Actually, after iteration 3: Plant C remaining supply = 10 − 9 = 1. After A-4 = 6 and B-4 = 1, demand Site 4 = 0. Total demand remaining = 0. But total supply remaining = 1 (Plant C).

This means the problem is being misallocated. Let me recheck iteration 3.

Wait, I made an arithmetic error. Let me redo:

After iteration 2: Plant B supply = 2, Plant C supply = 10, Plant A supply = 6. Total supply = 18. Demands: Site 3 = 9, Site 4 = 7. Total demand = 16. **Mismatch!**

Oh, I see the issue. After Plant B-2 = 10 was allocated, but Plant B had supply 12, so B has 2 left. But Site 2 had demand 10 — only exhausted. Plant A had 14 supply, allocated 8 to Site 1, so A has 6 left. Plant C has 10 left.

Total remaining supply = 6 + 2 + 10 = 18. Total remaining demand = 9 + 7 = 16. **Not equal!**

Hmm, this means there's an error in my tracking. Let me redo carefully.

Actually wait — let me restart more carefully.

Initial: Supply A=14, B=12, C=10. Demand: S1=8, S2=10, S3=9, S4=7. Total = 36 each.

**Iter 1 VAM:** Highest penalty = 2 (tied). Choose Row A's lowest = Cell (A, S1) cost 4. Allocate $\min(14, 8) = 8$.
- A's supply: 14 - 8 = 6
- S1's demand: 8 - 8 = 0 (S1 closed)
- B's supply: 12, C's supply: 10
- Demands remaining: S2=10, S3=9, S4=7

**Iter 2 VAM:** Compute penalties for the reduced 3×3 matrix:

| | S2 | S3 | S4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 8 | 8 | 6 |
| B | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| C | 4 | 6 | 9 |

Row penalties (sort each row ascending):
- A: sorted = {6, 8, 8} → penalty = 8 − 6 = **2**
- B: sorted = {3, 5, 7} → penalty = 5 − 3 = **2**
- C: sorted = {4, 6, 9} → penalty = 6 − 4 = **2**

Column penalties:
- S2: {3, 4, 8} → 4 − 3 = **1**
- S3: {5, 6, 8} → 6 − 5 = **1**
- S4: {6, 7, 9} → 7 − 6 = **1**

All row penalties = 2, all column penalties = 1. Maximum = 2 (Rows). **Tie-break:** Among rows A, B, C, pick the one with lowest cost cell. Row B has cost 3 (B-S2), lowest among all three rows. So pick **Row B** → cell (B, S2) with cost 3.

Allocate $\min(12, 10) = 10$.
- B's supply: 12 - 10 = 2
- S2's demand: 10 - 10 = 0 (S2 closed)

**Iter 3 VAM:** Reduced 2×3 matrix (S3 and S4 remain):

| | S3 | S4 |
|---|---|---|
| A (6) | 8 | 6 |
| B (2) | 5 | 7 |
| C (10) | 6 | 9 |

Row penalties:
- A: {6, 8} → 8 - 6 = **2**
- B: {5, 7} → 7 - 5 = **2**
- C: {6, 9} → 9 - 6 = **3**

Column penalties:
- S3: {5, 6, 8} → 6 - 5 = **1**
- S4: {6, 7, 9} → 7 - 6 = **1**

Max penalty = 3 (Row C). Lowest cost cell in Row C = 6 (C-S3).
Allocate $\min(10, 9) = 9$.
- C's supply: 10 - 9 = 1
- S3's demand: 9 - 9 = 0 (S3 closed)

**Iter 4 VAM:** Reduced matrix (only S4 left):

| | S4 |
|---|---|
| A (6) | 6 |
| B (2) | 7 |
| C (1) | 9 |

All three cells in one column. Penalties: each row has only one cell, so penalty = 0 (or undefined, treat as 0). Pick the lowest cost cell = A-S4 with cost 6.
Allocate $\min(6, 7) = 6$.
- A's supply: 6 - 6 = 0 (A closed)
- S4's demand: 7 - 6 = 1 (S4 has 1 remaining)

**Iter 5 VAM:** 

| | S4 |
|---|---|
| B (2) | 7 |
| C (1) | 9 |

Pick lowest cost = B-S4 with cost 7. Allocate $\min(2, 1) = 1$.
- B's supply: 2 - 1 = 1
- S4's demand: 1 - 1 = 0 (S4 closed)

**Iter 6:** Remaining: B has 1, C has 1, but no demand left. Actually, we still have to allocate. Cell C-S4 = 0 (because S4's demand is 0). Hmm — wait. After Step 5, S4's demand = 0, so the column is closed. But B has 1 leftover and C has 1 leftover, with no columns.

This means the IBFS is degenerate at this point — but actually, let me recount allocations.

Allocations made: (A,S1)=8, (B,S2)=10, (C,S3)=9, (A,S4)=6, (B,S4)=1. That's **5 allocations**.

Required = $m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$.

**5 < 6 → Degeneracy exists!** Need to add 1 dummy $\epsilon$ cell.

> [!NOTE]
> In this case, we have degeneracy because in Iter 4, the allocation of 6 to A-S4 simultaneously exhausted Row A AND Col 4's remaining demand was not exhausted (still 1). So that's fine — but also in Iter 5, allocating 1 to B-S4 exhausted Col S4 (which had 1). So after Iter 5, only B has 1 supply remaining and C has 1 supply remaining. No columns left to allocate. Hence only 5 cells filled — degenerate.

But wait, B has 2 supply, allocated 1 to S4, so B has 1 left. C has 1 left. Total leftover = 2. No demand. This is genuinely degenerate.

To resolve: Add $\epsilon$ at a cell that does NOT violate supply/demand — typically at the intersection of an exhausted row/column. Since both Row A (after Iter 4) and Col S3 (after Iter 3) are exhausted, we can place $\epsilon$ at (A, S3) since both are closed. This is fine.

So IBFS becomes: (A,S1)=8, (B,S2)=10, (C,S3)=9, (A,S4)=6, (B,S4)=1, **(A,S3)=$\epsilon$**.

**IBFS Cost:**
$$Z = 8(4) + 10(3) + 9(6) + 6(6) + 1(7) + \epsilon(8)$$
$$= 32 + 30 + 54 + 36 + 7 + 0 = 159$$

($\epsilon$ cost = 0 since it's tiny.)

---

#### STEP 2: Optimality Test using MODI Method

Occupied cells (with allocations): $(A,1), (A,3), (A,4), (B,2), (B,4), (C,3)$.

Set $u_A = 0$.

From occupied cells, use $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$:

| Cell | Equation | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| $(A, 1)$ | $u_A + v_1 = 4$ | $v_1 = 4$ |
| $(A, 3)$ | $u_A + v_3 = 8$ | $v_3 = 8$ |
| $(A, 4)$ | $u_A + v_4 = 6$ | $v_4 = 6$ |
| $(B, 2)$ | $u_B + v_2 = 3$ | $u_B = 3 - v_2$ |
| $(B, 4)$ | $u_B + v_4 = 7$ | $u_B = 7 - 6 = 1$ → so $v_2 = 3 - 1 = 2$ |
| $(C, 3)$ | $u_C + v_3 = 6$ | $u_C = 6 - 8 = -2$ |

So: $u_A = 0, u_B = 1, u_C = -2, v_1 = 4, v_2 = 2, v_3 = 8, v_4 = 6$.

**Compute $\Delta_{ij}$ for all unoccupied cells:**

| Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i + v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, 2) | 8 | 0 + 2 = 2 | 8 − 2 = **+6** |
| (B, 1) | 6 | 1 + 4 = 5 | 6 − 5 = **+1** |
| (B, 3) | 5 | 1 + 8 = 9 | 5 − 9 = **−4** ⭐ |
| (C, 1) | 5 | −2 + 4 = 2 | 5 − 2 = **+3** |
| (C, 2) | 4 | −2 + 2 = 0 | 4 − 0 = **+4** |
| (C, 4) | 9 | −2 + 6 = 4 | 9 − 4 = **+5** |

**Most negative $\Delta = -4$ at cell (B, 3).** Solution is not optimal. This cell enters the basis.

---

#### STEP 3: Closed-Loop (Stepping Stone) and Update

Starting from entering cell **(B, 3)**, trace a closed loop through occupied cells:
- **(B, 3) → (B, 4) → (A, 4) → (A, 3) → (B, 3)** ✅ (loops back)

Mark signs: (+) at entering cell (B,3), alternating (−) and (+) along the loop.

Allocations on the loop:
- (B, 3) = +θ (entering)
- (B, 4) = 1 → becomes (1 − θ)
- (A, 4) = 6 → becomes (6 + θ)
- (A, 3) = $\epsilon$ → becomes ($\epsilon$ − θ)

**θ = min of allocations at (−) cells = min(1, $\epsilon$) = $\epsilon$**.

New allocations:
- (B, 3) = $\epsilon$
- (B, 4) = 1 − $\epsilon$ ≈ 1
- (A, 4) = 6 + $\epsilon$ ≈ 6
- (A, 3) = 0 (drops out)

Other cells unchanged.

**New IBFS Cost:**

$$Z_{\text{new}} = 8(4) + \epsilon(5) + 1(7) + (6+\epsilon)(6) + 10(3) + 9(6)$$

$$= 32 + 0 + 7 + 36 + 30 + 54 = 159$$

Same as before (because $\epsilon \to 0$, the change is negligible). Let me verify using formula:

$$\Delta Z = \theta \cdot |\Delta_{\text{entering}}| = \epsilon \cdot 4 \approx 0$$

So $Z_{\text{new}} \approx 159$.

---

#### STEP 4: Re-test Optimality

New occupied cells: $(A,1), (A,4), (B,2), (B,3), (B,4), (C,3)$. Six cells, no degeneracy.

Set $u_A = 0$:
- (A, 1): $u_A + v_1 = 4$ → $v_1 = 4$
- (A, 4): $u_A + v_4 = 6$ → $v_4 = 6$
- (B, 2): $u_B + v_2 = 3$ → $u_B + v_2 = 3$
- (B, 3): $u_B + v_3 = 5$
- (B, 4): $u_B + v_4 = 7$ → $u_B = 7 - 6 = 1$
- So $v_2 = 3 - 1 = 2$, $v_3 = 5 - 1 = 4$.
- (C, 3): $u_C + v_3 = 6$ → $u_C = 6 - 4 = 2$.

**Compute $\Delta_{ij}$ for unoccupied cells:**

| Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i + v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, 2) | 8 | 0 + 2 = 2 | +6 |
| (A, 3) | 8 | 0 + 4 = 4 | +4 |
| (B, 1) | 6 | 1 + 4 = 5 | +1 |
| (C, 1) | 5 | 2 + 4 = 6 | −1 |
| (C, 2) | 4 | 2 + 2 = 4 | 0 |
| (C, 4) | 9 | 2 + 6 = 8 | +1 |

**Most negative = −1 at (C, 1).** Continue iteration.

Closed loop from (C, 1): (C, 1) → (C, 3) → (B, 3) → (B, ?) → ... Let me trace:

- Start: (C, 1) (+)
- Move to occupied cell in same column or row. Column 1 has only (A, 1) occupied.
- (A, 1) (−) → same row, find next occupied: (A, 4) (+)
- (A, 4) (+) → same column, find next: (B, 4) (−)
- (B, 4) (−) → same row, find next: (B, 3) (+)
- (B, 3) (+) → same column, find next: (C, 3) (−)
- (C, 3) (−) → same row, back to (C, 1) ✓

**Loop:** (C,1)⁺ → (A,1)⁻ → (A,4)⁺ → (B,4)⁻ → (B,3)⁺ → (C,3)⁻ → (C,1)⁺

Allocations on loop: (A,1)=8, (B,4)=1, (C,3)=9.
$\theta = \min(8, 1, 9) = 1$ (at cell B, 4).

New allocations:
- (C, 1) = 0 + 1 = **1**
- (A, 1) = 8 − 1 = **7**
- (A, 4) = 6 + 1 = **7**
- (B, 4) = 1 − 1 = **0** (drops out)
- (B, 3) = $\epsilon$ + 1 ≈ **1**
- (C, 3) = 9 − 1 = **8**

Other cells unchanged: (B, 2) = 10.

New occupied cells: (A,1), (A,4), (B,2), (B,3), (C,1), (C,3). **Six cells, no degeneracy.**

**New Total Cost:**

$$Z = 7(4) + 7(6) + 10(3) + 1(5) + 1(5) + 8(6)$$

$$= 28 + 42 + 30 + 5 + 5 + 48 = 158$$

**Improvement of ₹1 (per 100 m³).**

---

#### STEP 5: Re-test Optimality

Set $u_A = 0$:
- (A, 1): $v_1 = 4$
- (A, 4): $v_4 = 6$
- (B, 2): $u_B = 3 - v_2$
- (B, 3): $u_B + v_3 = 5$
- (C, 1): $u_C + v_1 = 5$ → $u_C = 1$
- (C, 3): $u_C + v_3 = 6$ → $v_3 = 5$
- From (B, 3): $u_B = 5 - 5 = 0$
- From (B, 2): $v_2 = 3 - 0 = 3$

So $u_A = 0, u_B = 0, u_C = 1, v_1 = 4, v_2 = 3, v_3 = 5, v_4 = 6$.

**Compute $\Delta_{ij}$ for unoccupied cells:**

| Cell | $c_{ij}$ | $u_i + v_j$ | $\Delta_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (A, 2) | 8 | 0 + 3 = 3 | +5 |
| (A, 3) | 8 | 0 + 5 = 5 | +3 |
| (B, 1) | 6 | 0 + 4 = 4 | +2 |
| (B, 4) | 7 | 0 + 6 = 6 | +1 |
| (C, 2) | 4 | 1 + 3 = 4 | 0 |
| (C, 4) | 9 | 1 + 6 = 7 | +2 |

**All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ → OPTIMAL solution reached!**

**Final Optimal Allocation:**

| From → To | Quantity (×100 m³) | Cost (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| A → 1 | 7 | 4 × 7 = 28 |
| A → 4 | 7 | 6 × 7 = 42 |
| B → 2 | 10 | 3 × 10 = 30 |
| B → 3 | 1 | 5 × 1 = 5 |
| C → 1 | 1 | 5 × 1 = 5 |
| C → 3 | 8 | 6 × 8 = 48 |

$$\boxed{Z_{\min} = ₹158 \text{ (per 100 m}^3)}$$

---

### 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — Assignment Problem (Hungarian Method)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:**
> A civil engineering firm has **4 projects** (P1, P2, P3, P4) to assign to **4 site engineers** (E1, E2, E3, E4). The estimated time (in weeks) for each engineer to complete each project is:

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | 7 | 5 | 8 | 11 |
| E2 | 4 | 6 | 7 | 9 |
| E3 | 9 | 8 | 5 | 7 |
| E4 | 11 | 7 | 9 | 5 |

**Find the optimal assignment (minimum total time).**

---

#### STEP 1: Row Reduction

Subtract the **minimum element** of each row from every element in that row:

- Row 1 min = 5 → Row 1 becomes: 2, 0, 3, 6
- Row 2 min = 4 → Row 2 becomes: 0, 2, 3, 5
- Row 3 min = 5 → Row 3 becomes: 4, 3, 0, 2
- Row 4 min = 5 → Row 4 becomes: 6, 2, 4, 0

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | 2 | 0 | 3 | 6 |
| E2 | 0 | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| E3 | 4 | 3 | 0 | 2 |
| E4 | 6 | 2 | 4 | 0 |

---

#### STEP 2: Column Reduction

Subtract the **minimum element** of each column from every element in that column:

- Col P1 min = 0 → unchanged
- Col P2 min = 0 → unchanged
- Col P3 min = 0 → unchanged
- Col P4 min = 0 → unchanged

Matrix after both reductions:

| | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | 2 | **0** | 3 | 6 |
| E2 | **0** | 2 | 3 | 5 |
| E3 | 4 | 3 | **0** | 2 |
| E4 | 6 | 2 | 4 | **0** |

---

#### STEP 3: Try to Make Assignments via Zeros

We need to make **$n = 4$ assignments** such that each row and each column has exactly one zero.

Zeros are at: (E1, P2), (E2, P1), (E3, P3), (E4, P4).

**Perfect!** Each row has exactly one zero, and each column has exactly one zero. We can assign directly.

**Assignments:**
- (E1, P2): 5 weeks
- (E2, P1): 4 weeks
- (E3, P3): 5 weeks
- (E4, P4): 5 weeks

**Total time:**
$$Z = 5 + 4 + 5 + 5 = 19 \text{ weeks}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Assignment: E1→P2, E2→P1, E3→P3, E4→P4, Total = 19 weeks}}$$

---

#### Hypothetical Extension: If Zeros Don't Form Perfect Match

If after row/column reduction, we cannot find $n$ independent zeros, draw **minimum number of lines** (using rows and columns) covering all zeros. If lines < $n$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adjustment Rule:**
> Let $k$ = smallest uncovered element. Subtract $k$ from all uncovered elements, add $k$ to elements at double-covered intersections (covered by two lines). Leave singly-covered elements unchanged. Repeat the assignment test.

This is the classic **König's theorem** application in the Hungarian Method.

---

### 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 3 — Maximization in Assignment

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:**
> Profit matrix (in ₹ lakhs) for assigning 3 workers to 3 jobs:

| | J1 | J2 | J3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | 30 | 25 | 28 |
| W2 | 22 | 30 | 25 |
| W3 | 28 | 30 | 24 |

**Solve for maximum profit.**

**Step 1:** Convert to minimization by subtracting each element from the maximum (30):

| | J1 | J2 | J3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | 0 | 5 | 2 |
| W2 | 8 | 0 | 5 |
| W3 | 2 | 0 | 6 |

**Step 2:** Row reduction (all rows have 0 minimum):

Matrix unchanged.

**Step 3:** Column reduction:
- Col J1 min = 0
- Col J2 min = 0
- Col J3 min = 2

Subtract 2 from Col J3:

| | J1 | J2 | J3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | 0 | 5 | 0 |
| W2 | 8 | 0 | 3 |
| W3 | 2 | 0 | 4 |

**Step 4:** Try assignment: Zeros at (W1, J1), (W1, J3), (W2, J2), (W3, J2).
W1 has 2 zeros — conflict. Draw minimum lines:

Cover row W1 and column J2 → covers all zeros with **2 lines** < 3 = $n$.

Subtract smallest uncovered = 3 from uncovered, add 3 to (W1, J2):

| | J1 | J2 | J3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | 0 | 8 | 0 |
| W2 | 5 | 0 | 0 |
| W3 | 0 | 3 | 2 |

Now zeros: (W1, J1), (W1, J3), (W2, J2), (W2, J3), (W3, J1). Draw lines: Cover rows W1, W2 and column J1 → 3 lines = $n$. Try assignment:

- W3 → J1 (0)
- W2 → J2 (0)
- W1 → J3 (0)

**Assignments:** W1→J3, W2→J2, W3→J1.

**Profit:** $28 + 30 + 28 = 86$ lakhs.

$$\boxed{\text{Maximum Profit} = ₹86 \text{ lakhs}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Transportation Problem Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define TP with m sources and n destinations] --> B{Is Sum Supply = Sum Demand?}
    B -- NO --> C[Add dummy row or column with zero cost]
    C --> D[Balance achieved]
    B -- YES --> D
    D --> E[Stage 1: Find IBFS]
    E --> E1[NWCM]
    E --> E2[LCM]
    E --> E3[VAM - Recommended]
    E1 --> F[Check for degeneracy]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Allocations = m + n - 1?}
    G -- NO --> H[Add epsilon in exhausted row/column intersection]
    H --> I[IBFS Ready]
    G -- YES --> I
    I --> J[Stage 2: Optimality Test via MODI Method]
    J --> K[Set u1 = 0 and solve u_i + v_j = c_ij for occupied cells]
    K --> L[Compute Delta_ij = c_ij - u_i - v_j for unoccupied cells]
    L --> M{All Delta_ij greater than or equal 0?}
    M -- YES --> N[OPTIMAL SOLUTION FOUND]
    M -- NO --> O[Pick most negative Delta_ij cell as entering variable]
    O --> P[Stage 3: Form closed loop and stepping stone]
    P --> Q[Determine theta as min of negative allocations on loop]
    Q --> R[Update allocations: add/subtract theta]
    R --> S[Degeneracy check after update]
    S --> J
    N --> T[Report optimal allocation and minimum cost]
```

### 4.2 MODI Method Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Occupied Cells Matrix] --> B[Solve System: u_i + v_j = c_ij]
    B --> C[Initialize Reference: u_1 = 0]
    C --> D[Back Substitute All u_i and v_j]
    D --> E[Compute Delta_ij for All Unoccupied Cells]
    E --> F{Delta Check}
    F -- All Delta greater than equal 0 --> G[Stop - Optimal]
    F -- Some Delta less than 0 --> H[Select Most Negative Delta]
    H --> I[Form Closed Loop]
    I --> J[Identify Theta]
    J --> K[Update Allocations]
    K --> B
```

### 4.3 Assignment Problem Hungarian Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: n x n Cost Matrix] --> B[Step 1: Row Reduction - subtract row minimum]
    B --> C[Step 2: Column Reduction - subtract column minimum]
    C --> D[Step 3: Attempt Zero-Based Assignment]
    D --> E{n independent zeros found?}
    E -- YES --> F[OPTIMAL ASSIGNMENT]
    E -- NO --> G[Step 4: Cover all zeros with minimum lines]
    G --> H{Number of lines = n?}
    H -- NO --> I[Subtract smallest uncovered element k from uncovered]
    I --> J[Add k to elements at double-covered intersections]
    J --> D
    H -- YES --> F
    F --> K[Compute Total Assignment Cost or Profit]
```

### 4.4 Transportation vs Assignment Problem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Optimization Problem] --> B{One-to-one assignment?}
    B -- NO, multi-unit supply --> C[TRANSPORTATION PROBLEM]
    B -- YES, exactly 1 unit per route --> D[ASSIGNMENT PROBLEM]
    C --> C1[Use NWCM, LCM, or VAM for IBFS]
    C1 --> C2[Test optimality via MODI]
    D --> D1[Apply Hungarian Method]
    D1 --> D2[Row and Column Reduction]
    C2 --> E[Optimal Solution]
    D2 --> E
```

### 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: TP vs AP Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transportation Problem] --> A1[m x n Matrix]
    A1 --> A2[Supply a_i and Demand b_j]
    A2 --> A3[IBFS Methods: NWCM, LCM, VAM]
    A3 --> A4[Optimality: MODI u-v Method]
    A4 --> A5[Allocations: m + n - 1]
    B[Assignment Problem] --> B1[n x n Square Matrix]
    B1 --> B2[All a_i = b_j = 1]
    B2 --> B3[Hungarian Method Only]
    B3 --> B4[Row then Column Reduction]
    B4 --> B5[Allocations: exactly n]
    A5 --> C[Real World Engineering Applications]
    B5 --> C
```
