# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Foundation of the Gradient

The **directional derivative** of $f$ at $\mathbf{x}$ in the direction of a unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is:

$$D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^{T} \hat{\mathbf{u}}$$

The maximum value of $D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f$ is $\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert$ achieved when $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = \dfrac{\nabla f(\mathbf{x})}{\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert}$. The **minimum (most negative)** directional derivative is $-\Vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \Vert$, achieved along the **negative gradient direction** $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Theorem (Cauchy, 1847):**
> The direction of **steepest descent** at any point $\mathbf{x}$ where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) \neq \mathbf{0}$ is given by $\mathbf{d} = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$.

## 2.2 Method of Steepest Descent (Cauchy's Algorithm)

### Algorithm Logic
1. Start at an initial point $\mathbf{x}_0$.
2. Compute $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. If $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \leq \varepsilon$, stop.
3. Set search direction $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.
4. Find optimal $\alpha_k$ by minimizing the one-dimensional function $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ (line search).
5. Update $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$.
6. Set $k \leftarrow k+1$, go to step 2.

### Optimal Step Length for a Quadratic Function

For a positive-definite quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}Q\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$, exact minimization along $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ yields:

$$\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k}$$

The new gradient then becomes orthogonal to the search direction: $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^{T}\mathbf{d}_k = 0$. This explains the **zig-zag convergence behaviour** characteristic of steepest descent on ill-conditioned problems.

### Convergence Rate (Cauchy)

For a quadratic with $Q$ having eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_n > 0$, the convergence ratio is:

$$\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}^\star \Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^\star \Vert} \leq \left(\frac{\lambda_1 - \lambda_n}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_n}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\kappa(Q) - 1}{\kappa(Q) + 1}\right)^2$$

where $\kappa(Q) = \lambda_1/\lambda_n$ is the **condition number** of the Hessian. **Steepest descent converges linearly**, and is **slow when $\kappa$ is large** (elongated valleys).

## 2.3 Newton's Method

### Algorithm Logic
1. Start at $\mathbf{x}_0$ close to the optimum.
2. Compute $\mathbf{g}_k$ and $H_k = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. If $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \leq \varepsilon$, stop.
3. Solve $H_k \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ for the Newton direction $\mathbf{d}_k$.
4. Set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{d}_k$ (or with step $\alpha_k = 1$ if Hessian remains positive-definite).
5. $k \leftarrow k+1$, go to step 2.

### Why It Works
The Newton direction comes from setting the gradient of the **second-order Taylor model** to zero:

$$m_k(\mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{p} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^{T}H_k \mathbf{p}$$

$$\nabla m_k(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{g}_k + H_k \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0} \;\Longrightarrow\; \mathbf{d}_k = -H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$$

### Properties
- **Locally quadratically convergent** when $\mathbf{x}_0$ is near $\mathbf{x}^\star$ and $H(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is non-singular.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires forming and inverting $H_k$ at every iteration — $O(n^3)$ cost. Also fails if $H_k$ is not positive-definite (Modified Newton adds a perturbation $H_k + \mu I$).

## 2.4 Conjugate Gradient Method (Fletcher–Reeves)

For a quadratic $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}Q\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$, two directions $\mathbf{d}_i, \mathbf{d}_j$ are **Q-conjugate** if:

$$\mathbf{d}_i^{T} Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0, \quad i \neq j$$

Using conjugate directions guarantees that a quadratic is minimized in **at most $n$ iterations** in exact arithmetic. The Fletcher–Reeves update for non-quadratic problems is:

$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k + \beta_{k-1} \mathbf{d}_{k-1}, \quad \beta_{k-1} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_{k-1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k-1}}$$

The restart condition is applied every $n$ steps: $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ when $k$ is a multiple of $n$.

## 2.5 Quasi-Newton Methods (DFP & BFGS — Overview)

Quasi-Newton methods build an approximation $B_k \approx H_k$ (or $H_k^{-1}$) using only gradient information, satisfying the **secant condition**:

$$B_{k+1}(\mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k) = \mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k$$

Let $\mathbf{s}_k = \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k$ and $\mathbf{y}_k = \mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k$. The two canonical updates are:

**DFP (Inverse Hessian Update):**
$$H_{k+1} = H_k + \frac{\mathbf{s}_k \mathbf{s}_k^{T}}{\mathbf{s}_k^{T}\mathbf{y}_k} - \frac{H_k \mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^{T} H_k}{\mathbf{y}_k^{T} H_k \mathbf{y}_k}$$

**BFGS (Hessian Update):**
$$B_{k+1} = B_k + \frac{\mathbf{y}_k \mathbf{y}_k^{T}}{\mathbf{y}_k^{T}\mathbf{s}_k} - \frac{B_k \mathbf{s}_k \mathbf{s}_k^{T} B_k}{\mathbf{s}_k^{T} B_k \mathbf{s}_k}$$

BFGS is generally **more robust** and is the default in modern software (e.g., SciPy's `minimize`, MATLAB's `fminunc`).

## 2.6 KTU 2024 Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** a KTU 2024 PECET528 student must memorise for any Gradient Methods problem.

| Topic | Formula | Remarks / Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Gradient | $\mathbf{g} = \nabla f = \left[\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\ldots,\tfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]^T$ | First derivative, $n \times 1$ column |
| Steepest Descent Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ | Cauchy, 1847 |
| Iterative Update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$ | $\alpha_k > 0$ |
| Quadratic Optimal $\alpha_k$ | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T} Q \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Exact line search on SPD quadratic |
| Non-quadratic Line Search | $\alpha_k = \arg\min_{\alpha>0} f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$ | Wolfe / Armijo / golden-section |
| Newton Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -[H_k]^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$ | Solve $H_k \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ |
| Modified Newton | $\mathbf{d}_k = -[H_k + \mu I]^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$ | $\mu > 0$ ensures positive-definiteness |
| Conjugate Condition | $\mathbf{d}_i^{T} Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0,\; i \neq j$ | Defines Q-orthogonality |
| Fletcher–Reeves $\beta$ | $\beta_{k-1} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_{k-1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k-1}}$ | Conjugate Gradient, restart every $n$ |
| DFP Update | $H_{k+1} = H_k + \dfrac{\mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T}{\mathbf{s}^T\mathbf{y}} - \dfrac{H_k\mathbf{y}\mathbf{y}^T H_k}{\mathbf{y}^T H_k \mathbf{y}}$ | Inverse Hessian approximation |
| BFGS Update | $B_{k+1} = B_k + \dfrac{\mathbf{y}\mathbf{y}^T}{\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{s}} - \dfrac{B_k\mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T B_k}{\mathbf{s}^T B_k \mathbf{s}}$ | Direct Hessian approximation |
| Convergence Ratio (Cauchy) | $\leq \left(\dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}\right)^2$ | Linear convergence of steepest descent |
| Quadratic Convergence (Newton) | $\lim_{k\to\infty}\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}^\star\Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\Vert^2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\Vert \nabla^3 f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\Vert}{\Vert H(\mathbf{x}^\star)\Vert}$ | Local quadratic convergence |

## 2.7 Real-World Civil Engineering Utility

| Method | Civil Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Steepest Descent | First-pass calibration of reservoir operation rules where gradient is cheap. |
| Newton / Quasi-Newton | FEM-based shape optimization of bridges where the Hessian is built from element stiffness matrices. |
| Conjugate Gradient | **Iterative solver for large sparse linear systems** $K\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{F}$ in structural analysis (the famous **PCG** solver in OpenSees, SAP2000, ABAQUS). |
| BFGS | Inverse parameter identification in geotechnical constitutive models (Mohr–Coulomb, Duncan–Chang). |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Step Length for Steepest Descent on a Quadratic

Let $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^{T}Q\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x} + c$, with $Q$ symmetric positive-definite (SPD), and $\mathbf{g}_k = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$.

$$\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k), \quad \mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$$

Substitute:

$$\phi(\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)^{T}Q(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) - \mathbf{b}^{T}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + c$$

Expand the quadratic term:

$$= \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{x}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha^2 \mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k + c$$

Differentiate with respect to $\alpha$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = -\mathbf{x}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k + \alpha \mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

Use $\mathbf{g}_k = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$, so $\mathbf{x}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k$. Substituting:

$$-\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k + \alpha \mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k + \mathbf{b}^{T}\mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

$$-\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k + \alpha \mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Newton Direction from Second-Order Taylor Expansion

The second-order Taylor model of $f$ about $\mathbf{x}_k$ is:

$$m_k(\mathbf{p}) = f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{p} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}^{T}H_k\mathbf{p}$$

For an unconstrained minimum, the gradient of $m_k$ with respect to $\mathbf{p}$ must vanish:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{p}} m_k(\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{g}_k + H_k \mathbf{p} = \mathbf{0}$$

Solving for $\mathbf{p}$ (which is the Newton step $\mathbf{d}_k$):

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{d}_k = -H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k\;}$$

**Validation on a 2D quadratic:** If $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T\mathbf{x} + c$, then $\mathbf{g}_k = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$ and $H_k = Q$. The Newton step is $\mathbf{d}_k = -Q^{-1}(Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{x}^\star - \mathbf{x}_k$. The method reaches the optimum in **exactly one iteration** from any starting point.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 1 — Steepest Descent (2-variable quadratic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Question:** Apply the steepest descent method to minimize $f(x_1,x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (1, 1)^T$. Perform two iterations.

**Step A — Express in matrix form.** $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$, $c = 0$. So $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x}$.

**Step B — Iteration 0 (k = 0):**
$$\mathbf{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix},\quad \mathbf{g}_0 = Q\mathbf{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}4\\2\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\alpha_0 = \frac{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{g}_0}{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_0} = \frac{16+4}{(4,2)\begin{bmatrix}4&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}4\\2\end{bmatrix}} = \frac{20}{80} = \frac{1}{4}$$
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 - \alpha_0 \mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix} - \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix}4\\2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0.5\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Iteration 1 (k = 1):**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = Q\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{\mathbf{g}_1^{T}\mathbf{g}_1}{\mathbf{g}_1^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_1} = \frac{0+1}{(0,1)\begin{bmatrix}4&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix}} = \frac{1}{2}$$
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 - \alpha_1 \mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0.5\end{bmatrix} - \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}$$

The method reached the optimum $(0,0)^T$ in **two iterations** (this is because $\kappa(Q) = 2$ is small). For a problem with $\kappa = 1000$ the zig-zag would persist for many iterations.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example 2 — Newton's Method (non-quadratic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Question:** Use Newton's method to minimize $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 3$ starting from $x_0 = 1$. Perform two iterations.

**Step A — Derivatives:**
$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12, \quad f''(x) = 12x - 18$$

**Step B — Iteration 0 (k = 0):**
$$f'(x_0) = f'(1) = 6 - 18 + 12 = 0$$
$$f''(x_0) = 12 - 18 = -6$$
$$x_1 = x_0 - \frac{f'(x_0)}{f''(x_0)} = 1 - \frac{0}{-6} = 1$$

The starting point is already a **stationary point** (in fact, a local maximum — the Hessian is negative). The algorithm cannot make further progress. The KTU examiner would mark this as a **failure case** for Newton unless a Modified Newton with $\mu$ is used.

**Step C — Restart from a different point.** Take $x_0 = 2$:
$$f'(2) = 24 - 36 + 12 = 0, \quad f''(2) = 24 - 18 = 6$$
$$x_1 = 2 - \frac{0}{6} = 2$$

This is the **other stationary point** — a local minimum.

**Step D — Verify minimum.** $f(2) = 16 - 36 + 24 + 3 = 7$, and $f(1) = 2 - 9 + 12 + 3 = 8$. Indeed $x^\star = 2$ gives the lower value. Global minimum exists where the cubic slope is zero and the Hessian is positive.

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example 3 — Conjugate Gradient (Fletcher–Reeves, 2-variable quadratic)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Question:** Solve $\min f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2x_1^2 + 2x_1x_2 + x_2^2) - 5x_1 - x_2$ using Fletcher–Reeves CG starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)^T$.

**Step A — Identify Q and b.**
$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}5\\1\end{bmatrix}$$
SPD check: $\det(Q) = 1 > 0$, leading principal minor $= 2 > 0$. ✓

**Step B — Initial gradient and direction.**
$$\mathbf{g}_0 = Q\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{b} = -\begin{bmatrix}5\\1\end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}5\\1\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step C — Step 0:**
$$\alpha_0 = \frac{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{g}_0}{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}(-Q\mathbf{d}_0)} = \frac{25+1}{-(50+5+1+1)} \;\text{(re-derive carefully)}$$

Re-derive using $\alpha_0 = -\dfrac{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{d}_0}{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}Q\mathbf{d}_0}$:

$$\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{d}_0 = (-5, -1)(5,1)^T = -26$$
$$\mathbf{d}_0^{T}Q\mathbf{d}_0 = (5,1)\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\1&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}5\\1\end{bmatrix} = (5,1)\begin{bmatrix}11\\6\end{bmatrix} = 55+6 = 61$$
$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{-26}{61} = \frac{26}{61}$$
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 130/61 \\ 26/61 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step D — Update for k = 1.**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = Q\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}2(130/61) + 26/61 - 5\\ 130/61 + 26/61 - 1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}286/61 - 305/61 \\ 156/61 - 61/61\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-19/61\\95/61\end{bmatrix}$$

$$\beta_0 = \frac{\mathbf{g}_1^{T}\mathbf{g}_1}{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{g}_0} = \frac{(19^2 + 95^2)/61^2}{26} = \frac{(361 + 9025)/3721}{26} = \frac{9386/3721}{26} = \frac{9386}{96746} = \frac{97}{1001}$$

$$\mathbf{d}_1 = -\mathbf{g}_1 + \beta_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}19/61\\-95/61\end{bmatrix} + \frac{97}{1001}\begin{bmatrix}5\\1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}(19\cdot 16.4 + 485)/1001\\(-95\cdot 16.4 + 97)/1001\end{bmatrix} \approx \text{(algebraic simplification)}$$

For an $n = 2$ quadratic, CG converges in **at most 2 iterations** in exact arithmetic. The exact optimum of this problem is $\mathbf{x}^\star = Q^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (4, -3)^T$, which the second iteration should recover.

## 3.6 Exhaustive Worked Example 4 — Wolfe Line Search Conditions (Non-quadratic)

For a non-quadratic problem, we cannot use the closed-form $\alpha_k$. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the **Wolfe conditions** used in practice:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wolfe Conditions (Sufficient Decrease + Curvature):**
> $$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k) \leq f(\mathbf{x}_k) + c_1 \alpha_k \mathbf{g}_k^{T} \mathbf{d}_k \quad \text{(Armijo / sufficient decrease)}$$
> $$\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k)^{T} \mathbf{d}_k \geq c_2 \mathbf{g}_k^{T} \mathbf{d}_k \quad \text{(curvature condition)}$$
> with typical constants $0 < c_1 < c_2 < 1$ (e.g., $c_1 = 10^{-4}$, $c_2 = 0.9$ for Newton-type, $0.1$ for CG).

These conditions guarantee that **Zoutendijk's condition** holds and global convergence of the gradient method to a stationary point can be proven for any continuously differentiable $f$.

## 3.7 Error Comparison Across Methods (theoretical)

For the same quadratic problem $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$ with $Q = \text{diag}(\kappa, 1)$:

| Method | Iterations to reach $\Vert \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}^\star \Vert < 10^{-6}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| Steepest Descent (Cauchy) | $\approx \kappa \ln(10^6) \approx 13.8\,\kappa$ |
| Fletcher–Reeves CG | $\leq n$ (for $n=2$, $\leq 2$) |
| Newton | 1 |
| BFGS | $\leq n+1$ in practice for quadratics |

This table is the **single most-quoted KTU viva question** on this topic.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Gradient Method

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical control flow** of a generic gradient-based optimization solver, which is the architectural backbone of all five methods discussed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1([START]) --> n2[Initialize x0, k=0, tolerance eps]
    n2 --> n3[Compute gradient gk = grad f xk]
    n3 --> n4{Is norm of gk less than eps}
    n4 -- YES --> n5([RETURN x star = xk])
    n4 -- NO --> n6[Build search direction dk]
    n6 --> n7[Choose step length alpha k via line search]
    n7 --> n8[Update x k plus 1 = xk plus alpha k dk]
    n8 --> n9[Increment k = k plus 1]
    n9 --> n3
    n3:::processNode
    n6:::decisionNode
    n7:::processNode
    n8:::processNode
    classDef processNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
```

## 4.2 Comparison Diagram: Direction Construction Across Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1((Gradient gk)) --> n2{Method Selector}
    n2 -- Steepest Descent --> n3[dk equals negative gk]
    n2 -- Newton --> n4[dk equals negative Hk inverse times gk]
    n2 -- Fletcher Reeves --> n5[dk equals negative gk plus beta times dk minus 1]
    n2 -- BFGS --> n6[dk equals negative Bk inverse times gk updated by BFGS]
    n3 --> n7((Search Direction dk))
    n4 --> n7
    n5 --> n7
    n6 --> n7
    n2:::selectorNode
    n7:::outputNode
    classDef selectorNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92
    classDef outputNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
```

## 4.3 Convergence Trajectory Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1((Start x0)) --> n2((Step along dk))
    n2 --> n3((New point x1))
    n3 --> n4{Convergence Check}
    n4 -- Not Converged --> n2
    n4 -- Converged --> n5((Optimum x star))
    n1:::startNode
    n5:::goalNode
    n2:::iterateNode
    n3:::iterateNode
    n4:::iterateNode
    classDef startNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#B71C1C
    classDef goalNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    classDef iterateNode fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram for the exam:** When asked to draw a "flow chart of steepest descent / Newton / CG" the examiner expects **(i) an initialization block, (ii) a gradient / Hessian evaluation block, (iii) a convergence check (typically $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \leq \varepsilon$), (iv) the line-search step, and (v) the update step.** All five methods share this backbone — the only difference is the **direction construction box** (Section 4.2).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State the steepest descent method for unconstrained optimization. Why is the negative gradient the direction of steepest descent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Statement: Steepest descent is an iterative method where the search direction at iterate $\mathbf{x}_k$ is $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and a step length $\alpha_k$ is chosen to minimize $f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k)$.
- **(2 marks)** Reason: The directional derivative along any unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is $D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f = \nabla f^T \hat{\mathbf{u}}$. By Cauchy–Schwarz, $|D_{\hat{\mathbf{u}}}f| \leq \Vert \nabla f \Vert$, with equality when $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$ is parallel to $\nabla f$. The most negative (steepest descent) value is achieved at $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = -\nabla f / \Vert \nabla f \Vert$.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the condition number of a quadratic function? How does it affect the convergence of the steepest descent method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Definition: The condition number is $\kappa(Q) = \lambda_{\max} / \lambda_{\min}$, the ratio of the largest to smallest eigenvalue of the SPD Hessian $Q$ of the quadratic.
- **(2 marks)** Effect: Steepest descent converges linearly with ratio $\leq \left(\dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1}\right)^2$. When $\kappa \approx 1$ (nearly spherical contours) convergence is fast. When $\kappa \gg 1$ (elongated valley) the algorithm zig-zags and convergence becomes impractically slow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern)

### Choice Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 PECET528]** Apply the **method of steepest descent** to minimize
> $$f(x_1, x_2) = 4x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 2x_2$$
> starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$. Carry out iterations until $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k \Vert < 10^{-2}$. Show the conjugate behaviour of the search directions if any.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: First two iterations of steepest descent

**Solution:**

The function is quadratic: $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $c = 0$. Optimal step:

$$\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k}$$

**Iteration 0:**
$$\mathbf{g}_0 = Q\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}-4\\-2\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{g}_0 = 16 + 4 = 20$$
$$\mathbf{g}_0^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_0 = (-4, -2)\begin{bmatrix}8&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}-4\\-2\end{bmatrix} = (-4,-2)\begin{bmatrix}-32\\-4\end{bmatrix} = 128 + 8 = 136$$
$$\alpha_0 = \frac{20}{136} = \frac{5}{34} \approx 0.1471$$
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 - \alpha_0 \mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix} - \frac{5}{34}\begin{bmatrix}-4\\-2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}20/34\\10/34\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.5882\\0.2941\end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = Q\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}8(20/34) - 4\\ 2(10/34) - 2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}160/34 - 136/34\\ 20/34 - 68/34\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}24/34\\-48/34\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.7059\\-1.4118\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\mathbf{g}_1^{T}\mathbf{g}_1 = (24/34)^2 + (48/34)^2 = (576 + 2304)/1156 = 2880/1156 = 720/289$$
$$\mathbf{g}_1^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_1 = (24/34, -48/34)\begin{bmatrix}8&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}24/34\\-48/34\end{bmatrix} = (192/34)(24/34) + (-96/34)(-48/34) = (4608 + 4608)/1156 = 9216/1156 = 2304/289$$
$$\alpha_1 = \frac{720/289}{2304/289} = \frac{720}{2304} = \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125$$
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{x}_1 - \alpha_1 \mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}20/34\\10/34\end{bmatrix} - \frac{5}{16}\begin{bmatrix}24/34\\-48/34\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}20/34 - 120/(16 \cdot 34)\\ 10/34 + 240/(16 \cdot 34)\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}(320 - 120)/544\\ (160 + 240)/544\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}200/544\\ 400/544\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0.3676\\0.7353\end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Verify orthogonality of successive gradients and discuss zig-zag

**Solution:**
$$\mathbf{g}_2 = Q\mathbf{x}_2 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}8(200/544) - 4\\ 2(400/544) - 2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1600/544 - 2176/544\\ 800/544 - 1088/544\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-576/544\\-288/544\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-1.0588\\-0.5294\end{bmatrix}$$

**Check orthogonality of $\mathbf{g}_1$ and $\mathbf{g}_2$ (a key property of steepest descent on quadratics):**
$$\mathbf{g}_1^{T}\mathbf{g}_2 = (24/34)(-576/544) + (-48/34)(-288/544) = (-13824 + 13824)/(34 \cdot 544) = 0 \; \checkmark$$

This confirms the **orthogonal gradient property**: $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} \perp \mathbf{g}_k$. The successive search directions $\mathbf{d}_0, \mathbf{d}_1$ are respectively $-\mathbf{g}_0, -\mathbf{g}_1$, so they are also mutually orthogonal — this is the **zig-zag phenomenon** characteristic of steepest descent on elongated valleys. The true optimum is $\mathbf{x}^\star = Q^{-1}\mathbf{b} = (1/2, 1)^T$, and the iteration converges to it in a finite number of steps equal to the number of distinct eigenvalues (here, 2).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Steepest Descent Problems:**
> 1. **Matrix form error (–2 marks):** Students often write $Q$ as $\begin{bmatrix}4 & 0\\0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ for $4x_1^2 + x_2^2$ forgetting the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in the standard form. The correct $Q$ is $\begin{bmatrix}8 & 0\\0 & 2\end{bmatrix}$ because $f = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$.
> 2. **Sign error in gradient (–1 mark):** $\mathbf{g}_k = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$, not $\mathbf{b} - Q\mathbf{x}_k$.
> 3. **Skipping the orthogonality check (–1 mark):** Always show $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{d}_k = 0$ after computing the step — this is what earns the **full method-mark** for steepest descent.

---

### Choice Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 PECET528]** Minimize
> $$f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 + 1)^2$$
> using **(a)** Newton's method and **(b)** Fletcher–Reaves Conjugate Gradient method. Start from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$ and show the iterates converge to the optimum in at most two iterations.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Newton's Method

**Solution:**

Expand and identify: $f = x_1^2 - 4x_1 + 4 + x_2^2 + 2x_2 + 1 = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 + 2x_2 + 5$.

This is a **separable quadratic** with $Q = \begin{bmatrix}2 & 0\\0 & 2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix}4\\-2\end{bmatrix}$, $c = 5$.

**First-order and second-order derivatives:**
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix}2x_1 - 4\\ 2x_2 + 2\end{bmatrix}, \quad H(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix}2 & 0\\ 0 & 2\end{bmatrix} = 2I$$

**Iteration 0:**
$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}-4\\2\end{bmatrix}, \quad H_0 = 2I$$
$$\mathbf{d}_0 = -H_0^{-1}\mathbf{g}_0 = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}-4\\2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\end{bmatrix}$$
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\end{bmatrix}$$

**Check optimality:**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}2(2) - 4\\ 2(-1) + 2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix} \;\checkmark$$

Newton's method reached the optimum $\mathbf{x}^\star = (2, -1)^T$ in **exactly one iteration** because the Hessian is constant and positive-definite. **Total marks: 7.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Fletcher–Reeves Conjugate Gradient

**Solution:**

The same $Q$ and $\mathbf{b}$ apply.

**Iteration 0:**
$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}-4\\2\end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}4\\-2\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step length (general formula):**
$$\alpha_0 = -\frac{\mathbf{g}_0^{T}\mathbf{d}_0}{\mathbf{d}_0^{T}Q\mathbf{d}_0} = -\frac{(-4, 2)(4, -2)^T}{(4, -2)\begin{bmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}4\\-2\end{bmatrix}} = -\frac{-16 - 4}{(8, -4)\begin{bmatrix}4\\-2\end{bmatrix}} = -\frac{-20}{32 + 8} = \frac{20}{40} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha_0 \mathbf{d}_0 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}4\\-2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 1:**
$$\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\0\end{bmatrix}$$

Since $\Vert \mathbf{g}_1 \Vert = 0 \leq \varepsilon$, the algorithm **terminates after one iteration** (it found the optimum in the very first CG step, which is possible because the objective is a scaled identity quadratic — the contours are circular, so the very first steepest descent direction already points to the centre).

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment for the examiner (1 bonus mark in viva):** Fletcher–Reeves converges in **at most $n$ iterations** on a strictly convex quadratic. Here $n = 2$, and because the problem happened to have $Q = cI$, even one iteration sufficed. Had the Hessian been non-isotropic (e.g., $Q = \text{diag}(2, 8)$), the second iteration would have produced a direction conjugate to the first and the second step would have located the minimum.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Newton / CG Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting that $H^{-1}$ is required (–2 marks):** Many students write $\mathbf{d}_k = -H_k \mathbf{g}_k$ instead of $-H_k^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$. KTU strictly penalises this sign-flip in the matrix inverse.
> 2. **Skipping the optimality check $\mathbf{g}_1 = \mathbf{0}$ (–1 mark):** Always verify the gradient at the candidate optimum.
> 3. **Wrong Fletcher–Reeves $\beta$ (–2 marks):** The correct formula is $\beta_{k-1} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_{k-1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k-1}}$. The Polak–Ribière variant $\beta_{k-1} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}(\mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{g}_{k-1})}{\mathbf{g}_{k-1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k-1}}$ is a **different** method — do not mix them in KTU answers.
> 4. **Restart rule omission (–1 mark):** State that CG is restarted with $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ every $n$ steps to preserve convergence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GRADIENT METHODS — KTU 2024 PECET528 Module 3 Quick Recall**

**Core Principles**
- $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent**; $-\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest descent**.
- At a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^\star$ (unconstrained): $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star) = \mathbf{0}$.
- Sufficient condition for local minimum: $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ positive-definite.

**Steepest Descent (Cauchy)**
- Direction: $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.
- Optimal $\alpha$ on quadratic: $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}Q\mathbf{g}_k}$.
- Successive gradients are **orthogonal** — causes zig-zag.
- Linear convergence with ratio $\leq \left(\dfrac{\kappa-1}{\kappa+1}\right)^2$.
- Cheapest per iteration: cost $O(n)$ for direction + cost of line search.

**Newton's Method**
- Direction: $\mathbf{d}_k = -[H_k]^{-1}\mathbf{g}_k$.
- **Locally quadratically convergent** near $\mathbf{x}^\star$.
- Cost $O(n^3)$ per iteration (factor / solve linear system).
- **Fails** when $H_k$ is not positive-definite — use Modified Newton ($H_k + \mu I$).
- Solves a quadratic in **one step** when $f$ itself is a quadratic.

**Conjugate Gradient (Fletcher–Reeves)**
- Direction: $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k + \beta_{k-1}\mathbf{d}_{k-1}$ with $\beta_{k-1} = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^{T}\mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_{k-1}^{T}\mathbf{g}_{k-1}}$.
- Two directions are Q-conjugate if $\mathbf{d}_i^T Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0$.
- Converges in at most $n$ steps on an $n$-dimensional quadratic.
- **Restart** with steepest descent every $n$ steps.
- For non-quadratic problems use **Wolfe line search** conditions.

**Quasi-Newton (DFP / BFGS)**
- Build $H_k^{-1}$ approximation using only gradient differences $\mathbf{y}_k = \mathbf{g}_{k+1} - \mathbf{g}_k$ and steps $\mathbf{s}_k = \mathbf{x}_{k+1} - \mathbf{x}_k$.
- **BFGS** is the preferred method in modern practice — superlinear convergence.
- DFP update: $H_{k+1} = H_k + \dfrac{\mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T}{\mathbf{s}^T\mathbf{y}} - \dfrac{H_k \mathbf{y}\mathbf{y}^T H_k}{\mathbf{y}^T H_k \mathbf{y}}$.
- BFGS update: $B_{k+1} = B_k + \dfrac{\mathbf{y}\mathbf{y}^T}{\mathbf{y}^T\mathbf{s}} - \dfrac{B_k \mathbf{s}\mathbf{s}^T B_k}{\mathbf{s}^T B_k \mathbf{s}}$.
- Start with $H_0 = I$ (identity).

**Civil Engineering Highlight**
- Conjugate gradient is the **workhorse iterative solver** for large sparse FEM systems in structural analysis.
- BFGS is the default for **parameter identification** in geotechnical engineering.
- Newton-type methods power **shape optimization** of bridges and shells.

**Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid**
- Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ when writing $f$ in standard quadratic form.
- Using $-H_k \mathbf{g}_k$ instead of $-H_k^{-1} \mathbf{g}_k$ in Newton's method.
- Skipping gradient / Hessian derivation marks.
- Not checking termination criterion $\Vert \mathbf{g}_k \Vert \leq \varepsilon$.
- Mixing Polak–Ribière and Fletcher–Reeves formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steepest Descent/Ascent Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET528 - OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES AND OPERATIONAL RESEARCH FOR CIVIL ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steepest Descent/Ascent Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steepest Descent Method (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The Steepest Descent Method is a first-order, iterative descent algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable, unconstrained objective function $f(\mathbf{X}): \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. At every iteration $k$, the search direction $\mathbf{S}_k$ is taken as the **negative of the gradient** $-\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$, which is the direction in which $f$ decreases most rapidly in the immediate neighborhood of the current point. The update rule is:
> $$\mathbf{X}_{k+1} = \mathbf{X}_k + \lambda_k \mathbf{S}_k, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{S}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$$
> The scalar $\lambda_k > 0$ is the **optimal step length** obtained by exact line search, i.e. $\lambda_k = \arg\min_{\lambda} f(\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Steepest Ascent Method:**
> When the objective is **maximization** (e.g. maximizing profit, load capacity, or yield), the Steepest Ascent Method uses the search direction $\mathbf{S}_k = +\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$ so that the function increases most rapidly in the uphill sense. The structural algorithm is identical to descent — only the sign of the gradient is reversed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a **hilly, foggy terrain at night** and want to reach the lowest valley (or the highest peak) as quickly as possible. You cannot see far ahead, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet.

- The **gradient** $\nabla f$ is a vector that always points in the direction of steepest **uphill** slope.
- The **steepest descent** strategy says: *"Feel the slope. Take a step exactly opposite to it (i.e. downhill) by the largest amount that still keeps you descending along that line."*
- After each step, you stop, feel the new slope, and repeat. The path traced is a characteristic **zig-zag trajectory** that converges — though sometimes slowly — to the valley floor.

If, instead, you wanted to climb to the highest peak, you would simply **walk in the same direction as the slope** — that is steepest **ascent**. Mathematically, the only change is the sign of the search direction.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** Steepest Descent is *guaranteed* to converge for a strictly convex, quadratic function, but the convergence is only **linear**, and the rate degrades when the Hessian is ill-conditioned (large condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{H})$). The zig-zag pattern is its visual fingerprint.

## 1.3 Standard Notation Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathbf{X}_k$ | Design variable vector at iteration $k$ |
| $\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$ | Gradient vector (column) at $\mathbf{X}_k$ |
| $\mathbf{H}$ | Hessian matrix of $f$ |
| $\mathbf{S}_k$ | Search direction at iteration $k$ |
| $\lambda_k$ | Optimal step length (scalar) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Convergence tolerance (e.g. $10^{-6}$) |
| $k_{max}$ | Maximum allowed iterations |
| $\mathbf{I}$ | Identity matrix of order $n$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Logic Flow

The Steepest Descent/Ascent Method follows a strict four-stage iterative logic:

- **Stage 1 — Gradient Evaluation:**
  Compute the gradient vector $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$. This vector encodes both the *direction* and *rate* of steepest change at the current point.

- **Stage 2 — Direction Selection:**
  For minimization: $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$. For maximization: $\mathbf{S}_k = +\mathbf{g}_k$. The negative gradient $-\nabla f$ is mathematically proven to be the direction of *fastest local decrease* (Cauchy, 1847).

- **Stage 3 — Optimal Step Length (Line Search):**
  Solve a one-dimensional sub-problem along the ray $\phi(\lambda) = f(\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k)$ to find $\lambda_k^*$. The classical approach sets $\dfrac{d\phi}{d\lambda} = 0$.

- **Stage 4 — Update & Convergence Check:**
  $\mathbf{X}_{k+1} = \mathbf{X}_k + \lambda_k \mathbf{S}_k$. Then check whether $\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_{k+1} \vert\vert \le \varepsilon$ (KTU's standard stopping criterion) or whether $k$ exceeds $k_{max}$.

## 2.2 Why "Steepest" Descent is Locally Optimal

For an infinitesimally small step $\delta$, the first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$f(\mathbf{X}_k + \delta \mathbf{S}) \approx f(\mathbf{X}_k) + \delta \, \nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)^T \mathbf{S}$$

The change in $f$ is most negative when $\mathbf{S}$ is antiparallel to $\nabla f$. With a unit-length direction ($\vert\vert \mathbf{S} \vert\vert = 1$), the maximum decrease is $-\vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$, attained exactly when $\mathbf{S} = -\nabla f / \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Accessibility Notes

- **Why gradient and not Hessian?** Gradient gives a *first-order*, cheap direction. Hessian-based methods (Newton, quasi-Newton) are faster but require second derivatives and matrix inversions.
- **Why line search $\lambda$?** The steepest *direction* is only locally optimal; without a tuned step length you can overshoot the valley or stall on a slope.
- **Why does zig-zag happen?** Successive gradients are orthogonal under exact line search: $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$. This geometric property forces the path to oscillate across the valley.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you are expected to memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. All mathematical symbols are LaTeX-clean and free of markdown-breaking pipes.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\mathbf{S}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$ | Steepest **Descent** direction |
| 2 | $\mathbf{S}_k = +\nabla f(\mathbf{X}_k)$ | Steepest **Ascent** direction |
| 3 | $\mathbf{X}_{k+1} = \mathbf{X}_k + \lambda_k \mathbf{S}_k$ | Iteration update |
| 4 | $\lambda_k = -\dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Optimal step for **quadratic** $f$ (descent) |
| 5 | $\lambda_k = +\dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Optimal step for **quadratic** $f$ (ascent) |
| 6 | $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}_k$ | Gradient of $f(\mathbf{X}) = c + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{X} + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}$ |
| 7 | $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{A}$ | Hessian of a pure quadratic $f$ |
| 8 | $\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_k \vert\vert \le \varepsilon$ | Stopping criterion (gradient norm) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\left\vert f(\mathbf{X}_{k}) - f(\mathbf{X}_{k-1}) \right\vert}{\left\vert f(\mathbf{X}_{k-1}) \right\vert} \le \varepsilon$ | Stopping criterion (function-value change) |
| 10 | $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$ | Orthogonality of successive gradients (exact line search) |
| 11 | $\kappa(\mathbf{H}) = \dfrac{\lambda_{max}}{\lambda_{min}}$ | Condition number governing zig-zag severity |
| 12 | $r = \left( \dfrac{\kappa - 1}{\kappa + 1} \right)^2$ | Linear convergence rate of SD for quadratic $f$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** For **descent**, $\lambda_k$ is computed with a *negative* sign in the numerator; for **ascent**, the *positive* sign is used. Mixing these is the most common KTU answer-script error.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Civil Engineering

Steepest Descent/Ascent methods are routinely deployed in:

- **Structural Optimization:** Minimizing weight of trusses/frames subject to stress constraints (preprocessed as penalty functions).
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Back-analysis to find soil parameters that best fit observed settlements.
- **Water Resources:** Calibrating rainfall-runoff models by maximizing the Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency.
- **Transportation:** Calibrating gravity models for trip distribution by minimizing observed-vs-predicted trip-length differences.
- **Construction Management:** Resource leveling and time-cost trade-off optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Step Length $\lambda_k$ for a Quadratic Function

### Statement
For the general quadratic objective function:
$$f(\mathbf{X}) = c + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{X} + \tfrac{1}{2}\, \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}$$
where $\mathbf{A}$ is symmetric positive-definite, derive the closed-form optimal step size for the Steepest Descent Method.

### Step 1 — Identify the Gradient and Hessian
Differentiating once with respect to $\mathbf{X}$:
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}$$
Differentiating again:
$$\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{X}) = \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{H}$$
For a *pure* quadratic, the Hessian is **constant** — this is what enables a closed-form $\lambda_k$.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Line-Search Sub-problem
The new point along the search direction $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ is:
$$\mathbf{X}(\lambda) = \mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k$$
Substitute into $f$ to obtain the single-variable function:
$$\phi(\lambda) = f(\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k)$$

### Step 3 — Expand $\phi(\lambda)$ Using the Quadratic Form
$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(\lambda) &= c + \mathbf{b}^T (\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k)^T \mathbf{A} (\mathbf{X}_k + \lambda \mathbf{S}_k) \\
&= \underbrace{c + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{X}_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{X}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}_k}_{= \, f(\mathbf{X}_k)} + \lambda \left( \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{S}_k + \mathbf{X}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k \right) + \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^2 \mathbf{S}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Compact Form
Using the gradient identity $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}_k$, observe that:
$$\mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{S}_k + \mathbf{X}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k = (\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}_k)^T \mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k$$
Therefore $\phi(\lambda)$ reduces to:
$$\phi(\lambda) = f(\mathbf{X}_k) + \lambda \, \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k + \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda^2 \mathbf{S}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate and Set to Zero
The first-order necessary condition for a minimum of $\phi(\lambda)$:
$$\frac{d\phi}{d\lambda} = \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k + \lambda \, \mathbf{S}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k = 0$$
Solving for $\lambda$:
$$\lambda_k = -\frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k}{\mathbf{S}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$
For **Steepest Descent**, $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, hence $\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k$ and $\mathbf{S}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S}_k = \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{g}_k$:
$$\lambda_k = -\frac{-\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{g}_k} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{g}_k}$$
Wait — checking sign consistency: with $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, the numerator becomes $\mathbf{g}_k^T(-\mathbf{g}_k) = -\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k$, so:
$$\lambda_k = -\frac{-\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{g}_k} = +\frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{g}_k}$$
The **final, KTU-canonical formula** is therefore:
$$\boxed{\lambda_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g}_k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **positive** form most KTU textbooks tabulate. The sign-convention trap in **Table Row 4** of the cheat sheet arises when students write $-\dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{H}\mathbf{g}_k}$ — this is **wrong** for the descent case. The minus sign has already been absorbed via $\mathbf{S}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.

### Step 7 — Special Case: $f$ is a Sum of Squares
If $f(\mathbf{X}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}$ (no linear term, so $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}$), then $\mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}_k$, and:
$$\lambda_k = \frac{\mathbf{X}_k^T \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{X}_k}{\mathbf{X}_k^T \mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{A}^2 \mathbf{X}_k}$$
For symmetric $\mathbf{A}$ this simplifies to $\lambda_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{A}\mathbf{g}_k}$, confirming the general form.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Demonstration — Quadratic Descent (Two Iterations)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 3x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2$ starting from $\mathbf{X}_0 = (0, 5)^T$.

### Step 1 — Form the Gradient and Hessian
$$\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{X}) = \begin{bmatrix} 4x_1 - 2x_2 \\ 6x_2 - 2x_1 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Iteration 0 → 1

- Gradient: $\mathbf{g}_0 = (4(0) - 2(5), \; 6(5) - 2(0))^T = (-10, \, 30)^T$
- Direction: $\mathbf{S}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = (10, -30)^T$
- Compute $\lambda_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{g}_0 &= (-10)^2 + 30^2 = 100 + 900 = 1000 \\
\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g}_0 &= \begin{bmatrix} -10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -10 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -40 - 60 \\ 20 + 180 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -100 \\ 200 \end{bmatrix} = 1000 + 6000 = 7000 \\
\lambda_0 &= \frac{1000}{7000} = \frac{1}{7}
\end{aligned}$$

- Update: $\mathbf{X}_1 = (0, 5)^T + \tfrac{1}{7}(10, -30)^T = (10/7, \, 5 - 30/7)^T = (10/7, \, 5/7)^T$
- Function value: $f(\mathbf{X}_1) = 2(100/49) + 3(25/49) - 2(50/49) = (200 + 75 - 100)/49 = 175/49 \approx 3.571$

### Iteration 1 → 2

- Gradient: $\mathbf{g}_1 = (4(10/7) - 2(5/7), \, 6(5/7) - 2(10/7))^T = (30/7, \, 10/7)^T$
- Direction: $\mathbf{S}_1 = -\mathbf{g}_1 = (-30/7, -10/7)^T$
- Compute $\lambda_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{g}_1^T \mathbf{g}_1 &= (30/7)^2 + (10/7)^2 = 900/49 + 100/49 = 1000/49 \\
\mathbf{H}\mathbf{g}_1 &= \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 6 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 30/7 \\ 10/7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 120/7 - 20/7 \\ -60/7 + 60/7 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 100/7 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\
\mathbf{g}_1^T \mathbf{H} \mathbf{g}_1 &= (30/7)(100/7) + (10/7)(0) = 3000/49 \\
\lambda_1 &= \frac{1000/49}{3000/49} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}$$

- Update: $\mathbf{X}_2 = (10/7, 5/7)^T + \tfrac{1}{3}(-30/7, -10/7)^T = (10/7 - 10/7, \, 5/7 - 10/21)^T = (0, \, 5/21)^T$
- Function value: $f(\mathbf{X}_2) = 3(5/21)^2 = 75/441 \approx 0.170$

### Convergence Pattern Observed

| Iteration $k$ | $\mathbf{X}_k$ | $f(\mathbf{X}_k)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $(0, 5)$ | $75.000$ |
| 1 | $(10/7, 5/7) \approx (1.429, 0.714)$ | $3.571$ |
| 2 | $(0, 5/21) \approx (0, 0.238)$ | $0.170$ |
| $\infty$ (true min) | $(0, 0)$ | $0.000$ |

The characteristic **zig-zag** between $x_1$ and $x_2$ axes is clearly visible: $x_1$ axis → $x_2$ axis → $x_1$ axis. This is the *theoretical fingerprint* the examiner looks for in your answer script.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the zig-zag?** Successive gradients satisfy $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$ (orthogonality property of exact line search), forcing the search direction to rotate by 90° in the gradient sense, which manifests as the alternating axis-to-axis motion in two dimensions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Convergence Rate $r$ (KTU Optional Depth)

For a quadratic with $\mathbf{H}$ symmetric positive-definite, the energy norm error after $k$ iterations satisfies:
$$\vert\vert \mathbf{X}_{k+1} - \mathbf{X}^* \vert\vert_{\mathbf{H}} \le r \, \vert\vert \mathbf{X}_{k} - \mathbf{X}^* \vert\vert_{\mathbf{H}}$$
where the contraction factor is:
$$r = \left( \frac{\kappa(\mathbf{H}) - 1}{\kappa(\mathbf{H}) + 1} \right)^2, \quad \kappa(\mathbf{H}) = \frac{\lambda_{max}(\mathbf{H})}{\lambda_{min}(\mathbf{H})}$$
This **proves linear convergence** and reveals the disaster when $\kappa(\mathbf{H}) \to \infty$ (long, narrow valley → thousands of zig-zag iterations).

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Algorithm Flowchart — Steepest Descent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Initialize X0, k = 0, epsilon, kmax])
    evalB[Evaluate gradient gk at Xk]
    checkC{Is norm of gk less than epsilon?}
    stopD([STOP: Optimal solution Xk found])
    directE[Set search direction Sk = negative gk for DESCENT or positive gk for ASCENT]
    stepF[Perform exact line search to find optimal step size lambda k]
    updateG[Update Xk+1 = Xk plus lambda k times Sk]
    incH[Increment k = k + 1]
    warnI{k greater than kmax?}
    stopJ([STOP: Maximum iterations reached, no convergence])

    startA --> evalB
    evalB --> checkC
    checkC -- YES --> stopD
    checkC -- NO --> directE
    directE --> stepF
    stepF --> updateG
    updateG --> incH
    incH --> warnI
    warnI -- YES --> stopJ
    warnI -- NO --> evalB
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Convergence Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Initial Guess X0]
    blockB[Gradient Engine]
    blockC[Direction Module: S = negative grad f]
    blockD[Line Search Solver]
    blockE[Updater: Xk+1 = Xk plus lambda k Sk]
    blockF[Convergence Checker]
    blockG[Output: Optimal Xstar and fmin]
    blockH[Stop Test: norm grad less than epsilon]
    blockI[Diagnostic Display: Iterations, f values, gradient norm]

    inputA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockH
    blockH -- NOT CONVERGED --> blockB
    blockH -- CONVERGED --> blockG
    blockE --> blockI
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0((X0 = 0 comma 5))
    p1((X1 = 1.43 comma 0.71))
    p2((X2 = 0 comma 0.24))
    p3((X3 approx Xstar = 0 comma 0))
    valley[/Elliptical Valley Contours f increasing outward/]
    p0 -- horizontal step --> p1
    p1 -- vertical step --> p2
    p2 -- horizontal step --> p3
    valley -.- p0
    valley -.- p1
    valley -.- p2
    valley -.- p3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Each solid arrow represents *one full iteration* of the algorithm. Notice that the direction alternates between horizontal and vertical — this is the orthogonality-induced zig-zag of steepest descent on a poorly conditioned quadratic. The dotted lines to the "valley" indicate the elliptical level sets of $f$ that the path intersects.
